title: Neurosymbolic AI: the 3rd wave
domain: neurosymbolic AI; trust; safety; interpretability; accountability; XAI
user_group: AI researchers and practitioners; stakeholders concerned with trustworthy AI
task: survey and synthesize principled integration of neural learning with symbolic knowledge representation and logical reasoning
model: hybrid neurosymbolic systems: neural networks + symbolic logic/reasoning; knowledge extraction; neurosymbolic cycle
data_modality: general (multi-domain); discussion spans vision, language, decision support
explanation_techniques: neural-symbolic integration; knowledge/rule extraction from trained networks (including confidence); symbolic reasoning layers; differentiable reasoning; constraints from extracted symbols
evaluation_design: survey/conceptual synthesis; taxonomy discussion (e.g., Kautz AAAI-2020); open challenges for the next decade
main_findings: Argues that trustworthy XAI needs neurosymbolic “knowledge extraction” to communicate learned rules, detect/repair bias (e.g., proxies under GDPR), and support human oversight; proposes a practical recipe: learn with neural nets, extract symbolic meaning, reason symbolically, feed constraints back to learning (neurosymbolic cycle), balancing approximate vs precise reasoning.
conclusions: Positions neurosymbolic AI as a coherent “3rd wave” direction combining System 1 (deep learning) with System 2 (symbol manipulation/reasoning) to improve explainability and accountability; calls for clearer definitions, shared taxonomy, and research into symbolic meaning emergence, commonsense, and robust knowledge extraction with uncertainty.
limitations: Non-empirical and broad; depends on progress in representations, extraction, and reasoning; limited standardized benchmarks; PDF appears to be an accepted manuscript version.

title: Interpreting Black-Box Models: A Review on Explainable Artificial Intelligence
domain: XAI survey; interpretability and transparency; responsible AI
task: survey XAI methods, taxonomies, evaluation frameworks, and challenges for interpreting black-box ML/DL models
model: n/a (survey)
explanation_techniques: reviews XAI families including feature attribution (LIME/SHAP), saliency/heatmaps, counterfactuals, rule-based and prototype/exemplar methods; discusses need for interactive and human-in-the-loop XAI; links to responsible AI and ethics/design principles
interaction_design: human–computer interaction emphasis (stakeholder needs, humans-in-the-loop, interactive XAI design principles)
evaluation_design: survey with hierarchical taxonomy, synthesis of evaluation frameworks and metrics, and discussion of challenges for enabling XAI
main_findings: Synthesizes XAI literature into an updated hierarchical taxonomy and discusses evaluation frameworks, emphasizing that interpretability requires stakeholder-appropriate explanations and that HCI/humans-in-the-loop considerations are under-addressed in many technical works. Highlights major challenges in adopting XAI (robustness, fairness, accountability, ethical and societal implications) and calls for goal-centric and interactive explanations rather than purely method-centric novelty.
conclusions: Positions XAI as essential for trustworthy deployment of black-box models in high-stakes domains; recommends multidisciplinary development (including HCI) and more formal evaluation/benchmarking to align explanations with user goals and responsible AI requirements.
limitations: Survey/secondary study; breadth makes completeness difficult and conclusions depend on included literature; many open questions remain on standard definitions, evaluation metrics, and how to operationalize interactive, user-aligned explanations across domains.

title: An incremental explanation of inference in Bayesian networks for increasing model trustworthiness and supporting clinical decision making
domain: Bayesian networks; explanation of reasoning; trustworthiness; clinical decision support
task: provide concise, incremental BN inference explanations identifying supporting/contradicting evidence and information flow
model: hybrid Bayesian networks (discrete + continuous nodes)
data_modality: clinical decision support case data (BN evidence variables)
explanation_techniques: incremental evidence-based BN explanation (supporting vs conflicting evidence); intermediate-variable “flow” via Markov blanket/influence chains; multi-level explanation output (verbosity levels)
evaluation_design: method paper with small clinician evaluation study
participant_type: clinicians (experienced; N not captured in excerpt)
metrics_trust: trust in model prediction (reported; no significant change with explanation)
metrics_understanding: clarity/utility judgments of explanations; similarity to clinician reasoning
metrics_performance: model decision impact assessed qualitatively; clinician assessment changes (reported)
main_findings: Finds explanation aligns with clinician-identified significant evidence and is meaningful, but does not significantly change model trust or clinicians’ decisions; does affect clinicians’ assessment and is appreciated yet criticized as too wordy/complex at deeper levels; suggests graphical enhancement and pruning with domain knowledge.
conclusions: Concludes concise incremental explanations can be generated quickly without user input for hybrid BNs; recommends simplifying/graphifying explanations and extending to temporal BNs and real-time evaluations under time pressure.
limitations: Small evaluation and case selection (mostly aligned with clinician expectations) may limit observed effects; experienced clinicians may benefit less; explanation generation may miss combined evidence effects due to one-at-a-time removal heuristic.

title: Never tell me the odds: Investigating pro-hoc explanations in medical decision making
domain: pro-hoc explanations; frictional AI; overreliance mitigation; radiology decision support
task: test pro-hoc explanations (examples for each outcome) in a human-first second-opinion protocol
model: explanations by examples / similar case retrieval support
explanation_techniques: pro-hoc explanations by examples (analogous cases per class) including counterfactual similar case; frictional AI design principle to introduce cognitive friction
interaction_design: human-first then second-opinion: clinicians decide first, then see pro-hoc examples for both initial and opposite outcomes
evaluation_design: controlled empirical user study with clinicians
participant_type: orthopedists
metrics_trust: (none explicit; focuses on confidence/utility rather than trust scale)
metrics_reliance: overreliance/automation bias mitigation goal via frictional protocol
metrics_understanding: perceived usefulness and confidence in diagnosis
metrics_performance: diagnostic accuracy (vertebral fracture detection)
main_findings: Finds pro-hoc example-based support is highly appreciated (especially by less experienced clinicians) and increases confidence, but does not notably improve diagnostic accuracy; argues controlled friction can foster more deliberate interaction and potentially reduce overreliance and long-term deskilling risks.
conclusions: Concludes frictional AI via pro-hoc explanations is promising for clinical DSS design and warrants larger, more powered studies; emphasizes human-centered protocol design grounded in user perceptions and participation.
limitations: Small sample size (participants and cases) limits generalizability; support can mislead depending on retrieved similar cases and case context; needs larger/real-world validation and careful sensitivity/specificity trade-offs.

title: Interpretable machine learning for dermatological disease detection: Bridging the gap between accuracy and explainability
domain: dermatology; interpretable ML; clinical decision support; SHAP; LIME
user_group: clinicians/dermatologists and ML practitioners (no user study)
task: classify dermatological diseases from patient records with an accurate hybrid model and provide local/global explanations
model: hybrid pipeline: SVM decision scores feeding XGBoost
data_modality: structured dermatology patient records (UCI dermatology dataset)
explanation_techniques: SHAP; LIME (local and global explanations)
evaluation_design: UCI dermatology dataset (366 instances, 6 classes); 80:20 train/test split; addresses class imbalance via oversampling; compares against SVM, Decision Tree, and XGBoost; reports accuracy/precision/recall/F1 and uses SHAP/LIME for explanation
participant_type: patient records (UCI dataset)
metrics_performance: accuracy; precision; recall; F1-score
main_findings: Hybrid SVM→XGBoost achieved higher classification performance (reported 99.26% accuracy) than individual models; SHAP and LIME provided explanations for feature contributions, supporting inspection for bias/irrelevant features and improving interpretability in a sensitive clinical task.
conclusions: Demonstrates a practical way to pair high-performing classifiers with explanation frameworks to bridge accuracy and explainability for dermatological disease detection and to support trust in model outputs.
limitations: Limited by dataset representativeness/diversity (e.g., population/skin-tone coverage); XAI explanations are approximations and may oversimplify nonlinear relationships; PDF appears to be a preprint/accepted manuscript copy.

title: An objective metric for Explainable AI: How and why to estimate the degree of explainability
domain: XAI evaluation; explainability metrics; objective measurement; legal/compliance framing
user_group: XAI researchers; model developers; end users in healthcare/finance
task: express and validate an objective, model-agnostic metric for explainability and test whether it correlates with user effectiveness
model: DoX/DoXpy: metric grounded in Achinstein’s Theory of Explanations (Ordinary Language Philosophy) using deep language models for knowledge graph extraction and answer retrieval; evaluated on systems using ANNs and TreeSHAP
data_modality: textual explanandum support materials (web pages, documentation, feature labels, XAI outputs)
explanation_techniques: DoX metric operationalizing Carnap adequacy criteria via archetypal Q/A coverage; uses/compares explanation sources including TreeSHAP and other AI outputs; interactive overview-based explanation (YAI4Hu) with open-ended Q/A; contrasts normal XAI output vs richer explanation corpora
interaction_design: interactive overviewing UI (click annotated concepts to open relevant overviews); open-ended question answering via search box (English) for YAI4Hu; comparison vs static two-level explainer and how/why explainer
evaluation_design: two user studies: (1) credit approval system with/without overview-based explainer (between-subjects, Prolific N=103) scored by multiple-choice quiz; (2) heart disease predictor comparing XAI vs how/why vs two-level vs YAI4Hu (students N=89); plus supporting experiments
participant_type: online participants (Prolific: 103) + Italian university students (89; CS, management for informatics, digital humanities, AI)
metrics_understanding: quiz-based effectiveness/understanding scores (multiple-choice; correctness)
main_findings: Higher DoX scores track richer, more accessible explanation corpora and align with higher user effectiveness on domain-specific quiz questions; DoX is deterministic and can serve as a cheaper proxy for user-study-based explainability evaluation, while still needing complementary measures for usability and correctness.
conclusions: DoX can support objective assessment of explainability (e.g., for iterative development and potentially compliance), but should be used alongside other metrics (truth/correctness, usability) and standardized thresholds remain open for regulation.
limitations: DoX alone cannot replace subjective usability/user-centrality metrics; sensitivity to choice of deep language model for pertinence estimation; cannot be normalized to [0,1] easily; does not account for correctness/truth of information; unclear application to government-to-citizen requirements and threshold-setting for compliance.

title: The coming of age of interpretable and explainable machine learning models
domain: XAI; interpretable ML; legal compliance; evaluation; transparency
user_group: ML developers, data controllers/analysts, regulators and domain stakeholders
task: connect interpretability/explainability strategies (post-hoc vs ante-hoc) with legal/regulatory requirements and outline evaluation and legal boundaries
model: post-hoc explainability methods and ante-hoc / interpretable-by-design model families surveyed; proposes assessment framing tied to proportionality and regulation
data_modality: tabular and imaging data focus (as stated limitation); broad application contexts including safety-critical domains
explanation_techniques: post-hoc explanations (e.g., SHAP-style plots, rule-based local explanations) and interpretable-by-design models; unified assessment perspective combining axiomatic methodological frameworks
evaluation_design: survey / conceptual analysis bridging technical methods and legal frameworks; provides recommendations and boundaries; no new experiments
main_findings: Highlights that legal requirements are abstract and not yet mapped to technical XAI standards; argues interpretability/explainability must be treated as compliance tools and tailored to domain/stakeholder scenarios; recommends multidisciplinary teams (ML + legal + social sciences), human-in-the-loop explanation delivery, and contingency planning due to jurisprudence uncertainty.
conclusions: Frames the “maturity” of XAI/IML as a socio-technical necessity driven by regulation; calls for clearer mappings from law to technical artifacts, better evaluation, and context-proportionate explanations that support trustworthy deployment.
limitations: Non-empirical and focused on tabular/imaging methods; regulatory interpretations may evolve; repository copy may differ from final publisher version.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning of Chemical Bonding at Solid Surfaces
domain: interpretable ML; materials chemistry; catalysis; scientific discovery
task: survey interpretable ML methods for understanding chemical bonding and surface reactivity
model: interpretable ML via feature engineering; algorithm development; post-hoc analysis (perspective)
data_modality: high-throughput experiments and quantum-chemical simulations (repositories) discussed; no new dataset
explanation_techniques: feature engineering for interpretability; interpretable model development; post-hoc analysis/attribution (general discussion)
evaluation_design: perspective/survey; no empirical evaluation
main_findings: Highlights that predictive ML in surface science is limited by black-box opacity; surveys practical interpretability approaches and argues that interpretability is pivotal for reliable scientific insight (e.g., chemical bonding understanding) rather than prediction alone.
conclusions: Positions interpretability as essential for accelerating catalysis/materials discovery and for building AI platforms that support human scientific reasoning and hypothesis generation.
limitations: [removed in public release: source-text extraction artifact or local-path reference]

title: Can Mind Perception Explain Virtuous Character Judgments of Artificial Intelligence?
domain: human-AI interaction; moral psychology; mind perception; trust/character judgments
user_group: general public participants evaluating AI vs human agents
task: test whether mind perception mediates weaker virtue/vice character judgments for AI vs humans
model: vignette-based experiments; mediation analysis
data_modality: vignette scenarios across virtue domains (truth, justice, fear, wealth, honor)
evaluation_design: two controlled vignette experiments with mediation analyses; Exp1 n=613; Exp2 n=584
participant_type: adult participants (vignette studies; details not captured in header excerpt)
metrics_trust: trust in AI via character inference (indirect; weaker virtue attributions)
main_findings: Character judgments of AI are systematically attenuated relative to humans, and this difference is explained by lower perceived mind (especially experiential mind); effects vary by virtue domain in exploratory analyses.
conclusions: Suggests a psychological mechanism (mind perception) for why people may be less willing to attribute moral character to AI, informing design/communication strategies for human-centered AI trust and accountability in ethically laden contexts.
limitations: Vignette-based and may not generalize to real-world interactions; relies on self-report measures and mediation assumptions; participant sampling details not captured in extracted header.

title: Public attitudes value interpretability but prioritize accuracy in Artificial Intelligence
domain: public attitudes; AI interpretability vs accuracy tradeoffs; high-stakes decision contexts
task: characterize how non-experts value AI interpretability across stakes, scarcity, autonomy, and accuracy; test revealed preference when interpretability trades off with accuracy
model: hypothetical AI systems described in vignettes; interpretability/accuracy manipulated (no specific algorithm)
explanation_techniques: interpretability framed as “explainable/understandable” AI; measured attitudes toward explainability; tradeoff framing between “more interpretable but less accurate” vs “less interpretable but more accurate” systems
interaction_design: scenario-based survey studies with ratings of importance and forced-choice preferences; manipulates stakes, scarcity, autonomy (recommend vs decide), and accuracy across studies
evaluation_design: 7 online studies (MTurk/Prolific), using mixed-effects ordinal regression and experimental manipulations; evaluates stability across applications and interaction with stakes/scarcity; includes within- and between-subject tradeoff designs
participant_type: non-expert online participants (US and UK samples)
metrics_trust: n/a (trust theorized as explanation-dependent; outcome is importance ratings for explainability)
metrics_reliance: preference/choice under interpretability–accuracy tradeoff (revealed attitudes)
main_findings: People endorse interpretability as important (especially when stakes are high and resources scarce) but are willing to sacrifice interpretability for accuracy when forced to trade off; this pattern can legitimize deployment of opaque high-accuracy systems in precisely the most ethically consequential contexts.
conclusions: Policy and design debates should treat “support for interpretability” as contingent: public may demand explanations in principle but prefer accuracy under pressure; aligning trustworthy AI with societal values likely requires mechanisms that avoid or reduce interpretability–accuracy tradeoffs in high-stakes contexts.
limitations: Findings are based on hypothetical scenarios and attitudes rather than real deployment; participant pools largely US/UK online samples (generalizability to other populations/stakeholders uncertain); tradeoff framing may not capture real-world multi-attribute decisions; does not measure stakeholder-specific perspectives (e.g., directly affected patients).

title: Dermatologist-like explainable AI enhances trust and confidence in diagnosing melanoma
domain: dermatology; melanoma vs nevus diagnosis; human-centered XAI for clinical decision support
task: evaluate impact of dermatologist-like explanations on diagnostic performance, confidence, and trust in AI support
model: ResNet50-based classifier trained to predict ontology concepts + diagnosis rule; baseline ResNet50; Grad-CAM localization
explanation_techniques: ontology-based concept explanations (clinically established dermoscopic features); multimodal local explanations (textual ontology feature + localized ROI via Grad-CAM thresholding); alignment via overlap metrics; temperature-scaled confidence labeling (“strong/weak evidence”)
interaction_design: web-based reader study across three phases; Phase 1 annotate features/ROIs + confidence; Phase 2 AI diagnosis only; Phase 3 AI diagnosis + explanations (text + localized ROI) with guidance video
evaluation_design: within-subject three-phase study (July–Dec 2022); evaluates diagnostic accuracy (balanced), agreement, confidence, trust; computes explanation overlap (Sørensen–Dice similarity) and ROI alignment; stratifies by clinician dermoscopy experience
participant_type: clinicians specialized in dermatology (82 board-certified, 33 residents, 1 unspecified)
metrics_trust: trust rating Likert 1–10 per case; phase comparison; trust correlated with explanation overlap
metrics_reliance: agreement with AI diagnosis; diagnosis change patterns across phases; advice-taking proxy
metrics_understanding: overlap between clinician-selected and AI-selected ontological features (Sørensen–Dice); ROI/heatmap alignment measures
metrics_performance: balanced accuracy; sensitivity/specificity; agreement rates
main_findings: Compared to conventional AI support, adding dermatologist-like XAI significantly increased clinicians’ trust in the system and their confidence in their own diagnoses, with strong alignment between AI explanations and clinician reasoning; diagnostic accuracy changes were small/non-significant overall and moderated by clinician experience.
conclusions: Domain-specific, human-like multimodal explanations can close part of the interpretability gap in safety-critical dermatology decision support and may increase willingness to adopt AI by improving trust and confidence while preserving competitive model performance.
limitations: Tested under artificial conditions; intentionally constrained explanations to be human-like (may sacrifice non-human pattern discovery); ontology creation/tailoring is domain- and user-dependent and may alienate some users; effects of individual explanation components not isolated; domain shift/generalizability not fully addressed and needs multi-center validation.

title: AI hybrid survival assessment for advanced heart failure patients with renal dysfunction
domain: clinical risk prediction; survival prognosis; interpretable ML for heart failure with renal dysfunction
task: build and validate an explainable survival assessment system and deployment-oriented tool for continuous risk monitoring
model: AIHFLevel: ensemble hybrid modeling framework (12 learners) + hybrid feature selection (filter/wrapper/embedded); conditional inference survival tree; SHAP explanations; website deployment
explanation_techniques: SHAP for global + local explanations; conditional inference survival tree for interpretable risk stratification thresholds; hybrid feature selection to reduce to 12 predictors; clinician-facing visualization of individualized risk profiles
interaction_design: web tool (www.hf-ai-survival.com) for clinician use: input key variables (13 queries) to get survival curves, risk stratification, and SHAP-based predictor contributions
evaluation_design: retrospective multicenter longitudinal cohorts with internal/replication/meta/external validation; compares against clinical features/biomarkers and established models using discrimination, calibration, DCA, and robustness analyses
participant_type: patients with advanced heart failure and renal dysfunction (retrospective cohorts; includes external BIDMC cohort)
metrics_performance: C-index/AUC; integrated Brier score; calibration curves; decision curve analysis; subgroup analyses
main_findings: AIHFLevel (12 predictors selected from many candidate schemes) outperformed existing models, clinical features, and biomarkers for survival/risk stratification across multiple cohorts and validation settings; SHAP-based explanations and stratification via conditional inference tree support interpretability and practical clinical use.
conclusions: An ensemble-based, feature-selected and SHAP-explained survival model can improve prognostic assessment and be packaged for clinical deployment to support continuous risk monitoring and individualized profiling for AHF/RD patients.
limitations: Limited ability to directly replicate/compare every prior modeling method due to heterogeneous feature engineering/parameters; observational design; needs prospective multicenter validation and randomized/controlled studies to test whether using the system changes decisions and improves outcomes.

title: Dermatologist-like explainable AI enhances melanoma diagnosis accuracy: eye-tracking study
domain: dermatology; melanoma diagnosis; objective evaluation of XAI via eye tracking; cognitive load
task: compare standard AI vs XAI support for dermoscopic diagnosis and quantify clinician interaction/cognitive load using eye tracking
model: explainable classifier adapted from Chanda et al. (diagnosis + characteristics heads; attention/ROI localization; polygon-based region indications); baseline AI without explanations
explanation_techniques: domain-specific explanations using established visual characteristics; explanation includes relevant characteristics and polygon-based region indications; objective eye-tracking measures (fixation count/duration) to assess interaction
interaction_design: cross-over reader study (AI phase then XAI phase on same images after delay); web-based webcam eye tracking (realeye.io) and onsite dedicated device validation (Pupil Labs Core)
evaluation_design: reader study with paired comparison of balanced accuracy (two-sided paired t-test, bootstrapped CIs); analyzes fixation counts vs agreement/disagreement and lesion difficulty; compares webcam-based study vs device-based validation
participant_type: dermatologists (web-based: 53 AI phase completers; 50 XAI phase completers; device-based validation: 25 dermatologists)
metrics_trust: trust/confidence discussed but primary evaluation is objective eye tracking rather than self-report
metrics_performance: balanced accuracy (macro avg sensitivity/specificity); fixation count and duration; correlations with experience and difficulty
main_findings: XAI increased dermatologists’ balanced accuracy by ~2.8 percentage points vs plain AI (n=76) and yielded objective interaction insights: disagreement with classifier predictions and higher lesion difficulty correlate with higher fixation counts (higher cognitive load). Trends were consistent across webcam-based and device-based eye tracking, though absolute fixation counts differed; experience correlated with fewer fixations (more efficient search patterns).
conclusions: Eye-tracking-based objective measures complement self-reported trust/confidence and can reveal when AI/XAI support increases cognitive load; XAI can modestly but significantly improve diagnostic accuracy over standard AI and should be designed to support clinician attention and sense-making, especially in disagreement cases.
limitations: Webcam eye tracking has lower precision/accuracy; eye tracking cannot explain reasons behind gaze; two-phase design may have memory/carryover effects; lacks real-world patient metadata; dataset includes biopsy-verified lesions (not typical prevalence of benign cases); diligence/attention differences between phases may confound measured accuracy; further prospective real-world studies needed.

title: Large language models encode clinical knowledge
domain: clinical QA with LLMs; safety and bias evaluation; trustworthy deployment
user_group: clinicians; lay users; AI researchers; policymakers
task: benchmark and human-evaluate clinical knowledge of LLMs; improve alignment for safer medical QA
model: PaLM (540B); Flan-PaLM; Med-PaLM (instruction prompt tuning); MultiMedQA benchmark (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU clinical topics, etc.)
explanation_techniques: human evaluation framework with axes: factuality, comprehension, reasoning, harm, bias; instruction prompt tuning for domain alignment; calls for grounding in authoritative sources and uncertainty communication
evaluation_design: benchmarking + human evaluation; compares model scaling and instruction tuning; discusses limitations and future directions
participant_type: clinicians and lay raters (pool size limited; often one rater per response)
metrics_trust: trustworthiness tied to safety/harm/bias; warns unsafe answers; emphasizes evaluation and alignment
metrics_understanding: comprehension, knowledge recall, reasoning scores; understandability/actionability considerations
metrics_performance: multiple-choice accuracy (state-of-the-art across datasets); human-rated quality/safety measures
main_findings: Introduces MultiMedQA and shows instruction-tuned LLMs can reach strong multiple-choice medical QA performance, but human evaluations show models remain below clinician expert level on clinically important axes (safety, harm, bias, etc.). Finds model scale and instruction tuning improve comprehension/recall/reasoning, but safe clinical deployment requires grounding in authoritative sources, uncertainty communication, multilingual support, and better safety alignment. Highlights fairness/equity harms and need for robust bias assessment beyond simplistic metrics; notes variability due to health literacy and rater background.
conclusions: Concludes LLMs show potential utility in medicine but current models have safety-critical gaps; robust human evaluation frameworks and alignment methods (e.g., instruction prompt tuning) are essential for creating safe, helpful clinical LLMs and responsibly translating research to practice.
limitations: Benchmark not exhaustive; English-only datasets; multiple-choice tasks easier than real workflows; limited number of human raters and responses with often single rater per response; bias evaluation limited and fairness harms complex/context-dependent.

title: Guidelines for clinical trial protocols for interventions involving artificial intelligence: the SPIRIT-AI extension
domain: clinical trial reporting; AI interventions; human-AI interaction transparency
user_group: trialists; clinicians; regulators; reviewers; AI developers
task: improve completeness/transparency of clinical trial protocols for AI interventions
model: n/a (reporting guideline)
explanation_techniques: SPIRIT-AI checklist items (AI-specific reporting: intended use, operational/development environment, input/output data, human-AI interaction, performance errors, etc.); companion guideline CONSORT-AI
evaluation_design: consensus guideline development + Delphi + pilot testing
participant_type: multi-stakeholder experts
metrics_trust: transparency and risk-of-bias appraisal for AI trials
main_findings: Provides 15 new reporting items to improve transparency and completeness of AI trial protocols; emphasizes describing human-AI interaction, integration setting, input/output handling, and error cases to enable rigorous prospective evaluation and trustworthy adoption.
conclusions: Concludes SPIRIT-AI will improve protocol reporting for AI interventions and assist editors/reviewers/readers in appraisal; recommends routine reporting of AI-specific items alongside SPIRIT 2013.
limitations: Guideline does not ensure trial quality by itself; adoption by authors/journals required; scope limited to interventional trials with AI components.

title: COVID-Net: a tailored deep convolutional neural network design for detection of COVID-19 cases from chest X-ray images
domain: medical imaging; COVID-19; deep learning; explainability audit
task: detect COVID-19 from chest radiography and audit model decisions
model: COVID-Net CNN with PEPX modules; evaluated on COVIDx dataset; explainability audit via GSInquire
explanation_techniques: GSInquire explainability-driven auditing; identification of critical factors in CXRs
evaluation_design: hold-out test evaluation on COVIDx; reports accuracy, class-wise sensitivity and PPV; compares against VGG-19 and ResNet-50
participant_type: patient cases (13,975 CXR images)
metrics_performance: Test accuracy 93.3%; COVID-19 sensitivity 91.0%; COVID-19 PPV 98.9%
main_findings: COVID-Net achieves strong benchmark performance and the explainability audit suggests predictions rely on clinically relevant lung regions rather than spurious artifacts, supporting model debugging and reliability assessment.
conclusions: Open-source, explainability-audited CXR models can accelerate development of practical clinical screening tools, provided continued data growth and prospective validation.
limitations: Benchmark dataset and labels evolve; early-release results may not generalize across hospitals/devices; explainability is machine-centric and not a clinical user study; not production-ready.

title: Symmetry prediction and knowledge discovery from X-ray diffraction patterns using an interpretable machine learning approach
domain: materials informatics; XRD; symmetry/space group prediction; interpretable ML
task: classify crystal system and space group from powder XRD patterns with interpretable features
model: tree-ensemble classification (Extremely Randomized Trees / ExtraTrees) on manually engineered peak features
data_modality: simulated powder XRD patterns summarized as 11 human-interpretable peak-position/count features
explanation_techniques: interpretable-by-design feature engineering; feature importance from tree ensembles; t-SNE visualization of feature space
evaluation_design: training on 199,391 simulated patterns; test evaluation on 46,068 patterns; reports accuracy/precision/recall/F1 and top-k candidate accuracy for space group
participant_type: simulated powder XRD patterns (training set)
metrics_performance: Crystal system accuracy 92.24% (ExRT); top-5 space group accuracy 88%
main_findings: Handcrafted interpretable peak features are sufficient for high symmetry classification accuracy, enabling knowledge discovery and robustness to missing/weak peaks via ensemble voting.
conclusions: Interpretable ML can reduce bottlenecks in early-stage crystallography by automating symmetry inference while retaining human-understandable decision logic.
limitations: Proof-of-concept uses ideal simulated patterns (noise-free, no broadening/overlap/impurities); real experimental data will require handling noise, peak overlap and out-of-range peaks.

title: Harnessing interpretable machine learning for holistic inverse design of origami
domain: origami engineering; inverse design; interpretable ML
task: infer human-understandable design rules for origami inverse design across patterns and objectives
model: decision tree + random forest rule extraction (tree branches as design rules)
data_modality: physics-based origami simulation databases (SWOMPS) with continuous/categorical design variables
explanation_techniques: interpretable rules from decision-tree branches; random-forest ensemble rule mining and F-score-based selection
evaluation_design: simulation-driven dataset generation; train/hold-out testing of rules; evaluates rule precision/recall and F-score across multiple design scenarios
participant_type: simulated origami designs (Miura-ori demo)
metrics_performance: Rule precision/recall and hold-out testing precision (example 0.86)
main_findings: Tree-based rule extraction yields sparse, actionable rules, handles categorical+continuous design variables, and enables multi-objective origami inverse design combining shape and non-geometrical performance.
conclusions: Interpretable ML can bridge simulation databases and practical inverse design by producing explicit rules designers can apply without running expensive optimization loops.
limitations: Results rely on simulator fidelity and dataset coverage; rules may not generalize to fabrication effects or high-fidelity FE behavior without retraining/validation.

title: Predicting and understanding human action decisions during skillful joint-action using supervised machine learning and explainable-AI
domain: human decision-making; joint action; explainable AI
user_group: behavioral/cognitive scientists and human factors researchers
task: predict future target-selection decisions in a joint-action herding task
model: LSTMNN (LSTM-layered ANN); SHAP for feature attribution
data_modality: time-series kinematic/state variables (positions/velocities; 50 Hz; 48 derived state variables)
explanation_techniques: SHAP (local and global feature importance for LSTMNN)
evaluation_design: model accuracy evaluation across prediction horizons (Thor=16,32); cross-expertise generalization tests; SHAP ranking comparisons (Kendall tau)
participant_type: human participants (10 novice pairs, 3 expert pairs; 40 novice trials, 48 expert trials)
metrics_performance: Accuracy 92.3–98.2% (overall mean 95.23%); cross-expertise accuracy drops (e.g., 40.68% expert->novice at Thor=16; 37.66% at Thor=32)
main_findings: LSTMNN predicts target selection well and reveals expertise-specific decision policies; SHAP suggests experts weight collective-state information (target heading and co-herder location) more than novices, and models fail to transfer across expertise, supporting distinct information-use strategies.
conclusions: SML+SHAP can be used to model and interpret human joint-action decision-making, potentially enabling research tools for understanding what information supports expert performance.
limitations: Approach assumes model inputs capture the information humans use and that feature-weight mappings reflect human information-decision mappings; task provides full global state visibility, so generalization to partial observability or other tasks requires validation.

title: Explainable AI via learning to optimize
domain: XAI; optimization; trustworthiness certification
task: build explainable and trustworthy inferences by embedding prior knowledge
model: Implicit learn-to-optimize (L2O) models + trustworthiness certificates
data_modality: optimization-based inference across tasks (signal recovery; CT imaging; arbitrage trading)
explanation_techniques: optimization-based explainability; trustworthiness certificates (pass/warning/fail)
evaluation_design: numerical experiments with PSNR/SSIM and certificate fail rates; demonstrates constraint/fidelity checks and failure detection
metrics_trust: certificate label rates (pass/warning/fail per property)
metrics_performance: CT reconstructions: PSNR 31.73 dB, SSIM 0.858; certificate fail rates (box/fidelity/data-reg) 0.00%/0.00%/5.70% (LoDoPab test set)
main_findings: Implicit L2O achieves the best CT reconstruction quality among compared methods while yielding the highest proportion of trustworthy inferences under certificate checks; certificates can flag inconsistent inference features and constraint violations in noisy settings (e.g., trading).
conclusions: Combining optimization-based model design with interpretable certificates provides a concrete mechanism for explainable and trustworthy ML in settings where domain priors and reliability checks are required.
limitations: Certificates depend on the chosen properties and thresholds and may not cover all failure modes; evaluation is demonstrated on a limited set of applications and does not include human-subject studies of explanation usefulness.

title: Modeling strength characteristics of basalt fiber reinforced concrete using multiple explainable machine learning with a graphical user interface
domain: civil engineering; materials; explainable ML
task: predict BFRC compressive, flexural, and tensile strength and explain predictions
model: Decision Tree, Gradient Boosting, LightGBM regressors + SHAP and LIME
data_modality: tabular mix-design and material parameters (10 input variables)
explanation_techniques: SHAP; LIME
interaction_design: GUI-based prediction tool; local and global explanations via SHAP/LIME
evaluation_design: train/test evaluation for three targets (compressive/flexural/tensile) with R2; compares explanation consistency across models and methods
participant_type: BRFC mix samples (tabular dataset)
metrics_performance: Testing R2: DT > 0.802, GB > 0.882, LGBM > 0.89; GUI LGBM trained on full dataset achieved R2 > 0.95 (learning phase)
main_findings: Different tree-based models can achieve strong predictive accuracy for BFRC strength, but SHAP/LIME explanations may disagree in feature importance order/magnitude, suggesting explanation method and model choice affect what is presented as “important” to users.
conclusions: Using multiple explainers across multiple models can expose explanation instability and supports building more trustworthy decision-support tools (including a user-facing GUI) for BFRC strength prediction.
limitations: Limited dataset can make explanations algorithm-dependent; SHAP and LIME can exhibit moderate contradictions, motivating expert evaluation (surveys/trials) to assess fidelity of explanations.

title: Improving explainable AI with patch perturbation-based evaluation pipeline: a COVID-19 X-ray image analysis case study
domain: XAI evaluation in medical imaging; robustness/bias detection; automated assessment without expert annotation
user_group: model developers; healthcare providers as end users (selection of XAI methods)
task: design an automated pipeline to evaluate post-hoc XAI methods for medical imaging by inducing controlled perturbation/poisoning and measuring explanation quality
model: deep learning COVID-19 X-ray classifier under poisoning retraining with static/dynamic triggers; evaluated XAI methods: backprop, guided backprop, GradCAM, guided GradCAM, occlusion sensitivity, ablation, LIME
explanation_techniques: patch perturbation / trigger-based evaluation; quantitative metrics: IoU, overlap difference (OD), trigger detection rate (TDR); pairwise consistency (mutual information, normalized cross-correlation, SSIM); runtime/efficiency; compares saliency methods (GradCAM family, BP family), perturbation methods (occlusion/ablation), and LIME
evaluation_design: experimental evaluation with multiple poisoning attack rounds using static and dynamic triggers; computes saliency maps and evaluates detection effectiveness, consistency across methods, and time efficiency; includes illustrative artifact case study referencing human-annotated ground truth comparisons
metrics_performance: IoU/OD/TDR vs trigger regions; consistency metrics (MI/NCC/SSIM); runtime; qualitative examples and artifact cases
main_findings: Finds no single XAI method dominates across all evaluation dimensions; BP/GBP and LIME achieve higher IoU in many trigger-detection scenarios, occlusion sensitivity and ablation show higher cross-method consistency, and gradient-based methods are generally faster; the pipeline can surface failure modes and guide method choice based on application requirements.
conclusions: Automated perturbation-based evaluation can reduce dependence on scarce expert annotations and provide a repeatable, multi-metric basis for selecting and improving XAI methods in medical imaging, aiding both developers and clinical stakeholders.
limitations: Triggers are synthetic and may not reflect real-world noise variability; lack of human-in-the-loop validation may limit ecological validity; only post-hoc XAI methods evaluated; future work to add inherent explainability, calibration analysis (ECE), real artifacts, and expert agreement/annotations.

title: Reliable water quality prediction and parametric analysis using explainable AI models
domain: water quality; potability classification; explainable AI
user_group: water quality managers and environmental engineers
task: classify water as drinkable vs not drinkable and explain key parameters
model: Random Forest (selected); Decision Tree; Gaussian Naive Bayes; Logistic Regression; SVM; SHAP-based explanation
data_modality: tabular water chemistry parameters (9 features; Potability label)
explanation_techniques: SHAP (Shapely explainer; global and local plots); mentions LIME as alternative
interaction_design: force/summary/dependency/decision plots for feature importance and justification
evaluation_design: train/test split 80/20; reports accuracy, precision, recall, F1; sensitivity/specificity; selects RF for XAI explanation
participant_type: water samples
metrics_performance: RF: accuracy 0.999; precision 0.997; recall 0.998; F1 0.999; sensitivity 0.998; specificity 0.999
main_findings: RF outperforms other models on the chosen dataset and SHAP visualizations identify influential parameters (e.g., solids, pH, sulfate, hardness) and their interactions for potability decisions.
conclusions: XAI can add transparency to water potability classification by explaining which chemical parameters drive predictions, supporting more informed water-management decisions.
limitations: Evaluated on a single dataset; performance may vary on other datasets with different features/instances; future work should use more complex and heterogeneous datasets.

title: Predictive modeling for breast cancer classification in the context of Bangladeshi patients by use of machine learning approach with explainable AI
domain: breast cancer; clinical prediction; explainable AI
task: classify breast cancer cases using supervised ML and interpret predictions
model: Decision Tree; Random Forest; Logistic Regression; Naive Bayes; XGBoost (best) + SHAP interpretation
data_modality: tabular dataset of derived image features (not raw scans) from patient records
explanation_techniques: SHAP (feature contribution/importance for XGBoost)
evaluation_design: k-fold cross-validation and standard classification metrics; SHAP analysis for interpretability
participant_type: patients from Dhaka Medical College Hospital (Bangladesh)
metrics_performance: Accuracy 0.97; precision 0.94; recall 0.95; F1 0.96 (XGBoost)
main_findings: XGBoost outperforms other classical ML models on the local dataset; SHAP provides insight into feature effects, supporting transparency and model trust for breast cancer classification.
conclusions: Explainable ML (XGBoost+SHAP) can support early breast cancer screening/classification workflows, but robust generalization requires larger and more diverse real-world datasets and clinical validation.
limitations: Limited dataset size and diversity; features are extracted image properties rather than raw scans, limiting image-level analysis; needs larger multicenter data and prospective validation.

title: Forecasting the strength of preplaced aggregate concrete using interpretable machine learning approaches
domain: civil engineering; concrete; strength prediction; interpretable ML
task: predict PAC compressive strength from mix/design variables
model: XGBoost (best) among 13 regressors; SHAP analysis; sensitivity analysis
explanation_techniques: SHAP; sensitivity analysis
interaction_design: GUI for compressive strength prediction
evaluation_design: model comparison across 13 regressors; reports correlation coefficient, R2, MAE, RMSE; includes validation/external validation discussion
participant_type: experimental PAC records (literature-derived; 11 input variables)
metrics_performance: correlation coefficient 0.9791; R2 0.9583 (XGBoost)
main_findings: XGBoost outperforms other models; sensitivity analysis ranks gravel (~44.7%) and sand (~19.5%) as major contributors; SHAP highlights water-to-binder ratio, superplasticizer, and gravel as key drivers of PAC compressive strength.
conclusions: Interpretable ML plus SHAP can support understanding and prediction of PAC compressive strength, and the delivered GUI can help practitioners apply the model without complex tooling.
limitations: Relies on a relatively small literature-derived dataset; generalization to new mixes/material sources is uncertain. Paper text contains an internal inconsistency on dataset size (261 vs 2871 in conclusion), suggesting reporting/processing ambiguity.

title: A novel framework based on explainable AI and genetic algorithms for designing neurological medicines
domain: bioinformatics; therapeutic peptides; explainable deep learning; multi-objective optimization
user_group: wet-lab peptide researchers and bioinformatics practitioners
task: discover and optimize neuropeptides for synthesizable neurological biopharmaceuticals
model: NPpred: BERT-NeuroPred (interpretable) + NSGA-NeuroPred (NSGA-II) with p-crossover/p-mutation
explanation_techniques: Captum-based interpretability (AA-level attributions); motif-based operators
interaction_design: web app for peptide design and exploration
evaluation_design: train/validation/test split (8:1:1) with accuracy/F1/AUC; ANOVA and Tukey HSD comparisons across model variants
participant_type: peptide sequences (6807 neuropeptides + 8647 non-neuropeptides from public databases)
metrics_performance: BERT-NeuroPred: accuracy 91.14%, F1 90.08%, AUC 97.12%; pilot app finds 205 candidate NPs (MPP>=0.90, length 20)
main_findings: BERT-NeuroPred outperforms prior SOTA on accuracy/F1/AUC; NSGA-NeuroPred refines classifier outputs into a smaller Pareto-optimal set using objectives such as predicted probability, molecular weight, and similarity to validated NPs.
conclusions: Explainable DL combined with genetic multi-objective optimization can accelerate discovery of synthesizable neuropeptides and reduce reliance on raw classifier probability for wet-lab selection.
limitations: Only a few objectives are optimized (future work suggests adding peptide properties like isoelectric point, amphipathicity); BERT-based models are resource-intensive and slow to run; broader design constraints may be needed for practical drug conjugates.

title: XElemNet: towards explainable AI for deep neural networks in materials science
domain: materials informatics; formation energy prediction; explainability
task: explain ElemNet formation-energy predictions and assess learned elemental interactions
model: ElemNet (formation energy from composition) analyzed via XElemNet (post-hoc + transparency methods)
data_modality: elemental composition vectors (86 elements) with DFT formation enthalpy targets
explanation_techniques: feature importance analysis; correlation-based rankings; convex hull/stability analysis on secondary datasets; decision-tree surrogate
evaluation_design: post-hoc evaluation on secondary binary/ternary datasets; qualitative alignment checks with chemical knowledge
participant_type: materials compositions (OQMD-JARVIS; 321,140 unique compositions; plus secondary ternary dataset 1,048,575 samples)
main_findings: ElemNet predictions largely align with theoretical expectations on stability across many element-pair systems; feature rankings emphasize chemically meaningful elements (e.g., halogens show expected correlations), increasing confidence in model grounding.
conclusions: A suite of post-hoc and transparency XAI methods can make deep materials-property models more interpretable and help identify both reliable regimes and areas needing refinement for materials discovery.
limitations: Limited to ElemNet and selected secondary analyses; future work needed for more complex composition spaces (ternary/quaternary), additional XAI methods (rule extraction, activation analysis), and broader model generalization assessment.

title: Explainable AI-based suicidal and non-suicidal ideations detection from social media text with enhanced ensemble technique
domain: mental health NLP; suicidal ideation detection; explainable AI
user_group: mental health professionals and platform moderators
task: classify suicidal vs non-suicidal ideation from social media text
model: ensemble techniques (e.g., Random Forest+LSTM; XGBoost+CNN) with SHAP explanation
explanation_techniques: SHAP (feature attribution/importance for text features); mentions LIME conceptually
evaluation_design: experimental evaluation on multiple datasets; reports accuracy/precision/recall/F1/AUC-ROC and time-to-explain measures
participant_type: Reddit posts (SuicideWatch-related; Kaggle dataset)
metrics_performance: F1 suicidal 95.5% and non-suicidal 99% (reported); dataset#2 accuracy ~0.872–0.891 and AUC-ROC 0.90–0.92 depending on ensemble
main_findings: Ensembling improves detection accuracy and SHAP explanations aim to reveal hidden linguistic patterns underlying predictions, enabling more transparent monitoring tools for intervention workflows.
conclusions: Explainability can increase trust and usability of automated suicidal-ideation monitoring by exposing influential features while maintaining strong classification performance on large social media corpora.
limitations: Sensitive domain with labeling uncertainty and dataset bias; model performance depends on platform-specific language and may not generalize; ethical handling requires caution and human oversight for intervention decisions.

title: Predicting cardiovascular risk with hybrid ensemble learning and explainable AI
domain: cardiovascular risk prediction; explainable AI; ensemble learning
task: classify cardiovascular disease risk from tabular risk factors
model: hybrid stacked ensemble (Gradient Boosting, CatBoost, Neural Networks; meta-learner XGBoost) + SHAP; PCA/t-SNE visualization
explanation_techniques: SHAP; PCA; t-SNE
evaluation_design: train/test split 80/20; reports accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC; discusses feature importance and clustering projections
participant_type: tabular patient records (combined public datasets)
metrics_performance: Accuracy 82%; Precision 81%; Recall 83%; F1 82%; AUC-ROC 0.82
main_findings: Hybrid stacking improves recall/AUC compared to base models; SHAP and low-dimensional projections highlight key drivers (e.g., blood pressure, BMI, cholesterol-glucose ratio) and support clinician interpretation of risk factors.
conclusions: Combining ensemble learning with XAI can yield clinically more trustworthy risk models by pairing competitive discrimination with interpretable feature contributions.
limitations: Trained/tested on a single merged dataset; generalization across populations/settings is uncertain; class imbalance and feature list limitations may bias results; base learners remain partially opaque and need further transparency/validation.

title: Deep learning driven interpretable and informed decision making model for brain tumour prediction using explainable AI
domain: brain tumour prediction; medical imaging; explainable AI
task: binary classification of tumour presence from brain MRI images
model: NASNet Large (fine-tuned) benchmarked vs CNN/VGG16/ResNet50/EfficientNetB3/DenseNet121/Xception; uncertainty via Monte Carlo Dropout; post-hoc visual explanations via LIME and Grad-CAM; PCA/t-SNE visual diagnostics
data_modality: MRI brain images (Tumour vs No Tumour)
explanation_techniques: LIME; Grad-CAM; Monte Carlo Dropout (uncertainty); PCA; t-SNE
evaluation_design: train/test split 70/30; compares multiple CNN backbones; reports accuracy and miss-rate; qualitative LIME/Grad-CAM visualizations; discusses uncertainty scoring
participant_type: brain MRI image dataset (binary labels)
metrics_performance: Accuracy 92.98%; Miss-rate 7.02% (NASNet Large fine-tuned)
main_findings: NASNet Large outperforms alternative backbones on the dataset; LIME and Grad-CAM highlight salient tumour regions to increase transparency; uncertainty estimates flag low-confidence cases for clinician review.
conclusions: Combining high-performing deep models with visual explanations and uncertainty estimation can improve the interpretability and perceived trustworthiness of automated brain tumour screening.
limitations: Relatively limited dataset size and single acquisition protocol reduce generalizability; LIME/Grad-CAM can be sensitive and inconsistent; no prospective clinical validation or multi-center testing reported.

title: The effectiveness of explainable AI on human factors in trust models
domain: human factors; trust in AI; science communication; technology acceptance
task: test whether perceived explainability affects trust in AI engineers (ability/benevolence/integrity) and downstream attitudes/use intention
model: for survey analysis: structural equation model (SEM) of perceived explainability → trust constructs → attitude → intention to use autonomous passenger drones
explanation_techniques: perceived explainability (self-report) as an XAI-related construct; discusses XAI communication and uncertainty reduction rather than a specific explanation algorithm
evaluation_design: public opinion survey; SEM with mediation/serial mediation analyses (MPlus)
participant_type: adults in Singapore (public survey panel)
metrics_trust: trust in AI engineers (ability/benevolence/integrity); trust in AI system (self-report)
metrics_reliance: intention to use autonomous passenger drones (tourism/daily commute/cross-country/city travel)
main_findings: Perceived explainability positively predicts trust in AI engineers across ability, benevolence, and integrity even after controlling for trust in the AI system. Trust in engineers’ ability then predicts attitude and intention to use multiple autonomous drone transport scenarios; several serial mediation pathways connect explainability perception to intention through trust in ability and attitude.
conclusions: XAI communication (or factors shaping perceived explainability) may influence not only trust in AI systems but also trust in the people behind them; for high-risk applications, explainability perceptions can help reduce uncertainty and support acceptance.
limitations: Cross-sectional design limits causal claims; explainability measure may partly capture individuals’ efficacy to comprehend AI rather than system affordances; responses are imaginative because passenger drones are not yet deployed in Singapore; results may not generalize beyond Singapore’s context and awareness.

title: Personalized health monitoring using explainable AI: bridging trust in predictive healthcare
domain: predictive healthcare; trust; explainable AI
task: predict clinical outcome/risk class from structured EHR features
model: PersonalCareNet (deep model over structured features) with attention and SHAP-based explanations; PCA-based preprocessing; compared against LR/RF/XGBoost/DNN/TabNet/AutoGluon/NODE/DeepGBM/EBM
data_modality: structured EHR (MIMIC-III subset; tabular ICU variables)
explanation_techniques: SHAP (summary/force/dependence plots); attention weights; PCA
evaluation_design: baseline comparison on MIMIC-III subset; reports accuracy/precision/recall/F1/AUC; includes ablation of attention and explainability components; qualitative SHAP analyses
participant_type: MIMIC-III ICU patient records subset (balanced classes)
metrics_performance: Accuracy 97.86%; Precision 96.2%; Recall 95.4%; F1 95.8%; AUC 98.3
main_findings: PersonalCareNet substantially outperforms strong tabular baselines on the curated MIMIC-III subset; SHAP explanations consistently emphasize age and glucose and provide patient-level rationale intended to support clinician trust.
conclusions: High-performing predictive healthcare models can be made more clinically actionable by coupling outcome prediction with explanation interfaces that support both global validation and individual-case review.
limitations: Evaluation is limited to a single (down-sampled) MIMIC-III subset; generalization to other hospitals/datasets and deployment constraints are untested; SHAP and deep models can add computational overhead; additional multimodal data and prospective validation are proposed as future work.

title: Clinician perspectives on explainability in AI-driven closed-loop neurotechnology
domain: closed-loop neurotechnology; clinical translation; explainability needs and trust
task: identify clinician informational needs and preferences for explainability to support adoption/trust of AI-driven closed-loop neurotechnology
model: n/a (requirements/needs study; discusses XAI options such as feature importance)
explanation_techniques: clinician-preferred explainability: input data provenance/quality/bias; feature importance/relevance; confidence/uncertainty; links from outputs to clinical outcomes; tiered/role-adapted explainability frameworks; suggests combining statistical XAI (e.g., SHAP-like) with case-based/clinical reasoning cues
interaction_design: semi-structured interviews (online/phone/in-person), reflexive thematic analysis; concept map organized by system levels (input/algorithm/output) and UI/XAI visualization desiderata
evaluation_design: qualitative requirements elicitation; purposive snowball sampling; data saturation at 20 interviews; thematic analysis with iterative review and translation of excerpts
participant_type: clinicians: 13 neurologists, 5 neurosurgeons, 2 psychiatrists (Germany n=16; Switzerland n=4)
metrics_trust: trust discussed as dependent on input data representativeness and alignment with clinical reasoning; emphasizes validation and accountability over “black-box mechanics”
metrics_reliance: clinician willingness to rely tied to demonstrated utility, outcome alignment, and procedural safeguards rather than deep algorithmic transparency
metrics_understanding: need for clinically meaningful explanations and usable interfaces; recognizes limited clinician AI training as barrier to understanding
metrics_performance: expects trust to be earned via clinical validation and performance/benefit; not a performance study
main_findings: Clinicians prioritize explanations about input data and clinically grounded output rationale; they largely deprioritize full algorithmic transparency. Trust is earned through context, validation, and alignment with clinical reasoning, with interest in feature importance and other pragmatic XAI outputs; supports tiered, stakeholder-adapted explainability approaches for regulation and design.
conclusions: Designing explainability for closed-loop neurotechnology should emphasize clinically meaningful, context-sensitive information (data provenance, feature relevance, outcome linkage) and user-centered interfaces, complemented by role-adapted explainability to meet stakeholder and regulatory needs while supporting clinician trust and reliance.
limitations: Qualitative findings not statistically generalizable; limited to 20 German-speaking clinicians from Germany/Switzerland; low female participation; interviewer positionality may introduce bias; not a proof-of-concept of XAI methods; clinician technical AI understanding varies.

title: Clinical validation of explainable AI for fetal growth scans through multi-level, cross-institutional prospective end-user evaluation
domain: fetal ultrasound; human-AI collaboration; prospective end-user evaluation and trustworthiness
user_group: clinicians performing fetal growth scans; expert raters (sonographers/fetal medicine specialists); obstetricians/trainees
task: develop and prospectively validate an explainable DL system providing actionable feedback (concepts + segmentations) for fetal growth scan standard planes
model: modified Progressive Concept Bottleneck Model; concept explanations (anatomical landmarks, image optimization feedback) + segmentation; quality ranking via confidence; uses retrospective DICOM + prospective full-length videos
data_modality: ultrasound images and videos (DICOM; full-length screen recordings)
explanation_techniques: concept bottleneck explanations using pre-established clinical concepts; segmentation outlines of anatomical landmarks; uses model confidence to select best frames (auto-capture) and to rank quality; contrasts with heatmap XAI (Grad-CAM) limitations discussed
interaction_design: real-time feedback concept; prospective evaluation with clinician end-users; no interactive editing of concept assumptions in study design
evaluation_design: prospective cross-center evaluation in 4 steps: (1) 36 expert clinicians rate auto-capture vs manual images (300 comparisons); (2) 2 experts validate correctness on 120 images; (3) 46 clinicians rate usefulness of segmentations/explanations; (4) compares AI confidence by operator expertise on 100 scans
participant_type: pregnant women (prospective recordings, n=122) and clinician end-users/raters (Step1 n=36 experts; Step3 n=46)
metrics_trust: n/a (trust proxied by perceived usefulness and clinical adoption readiness; focuses on correctness/usefulness)
metrics_understanding: clinical usefulness ratings for explanations/segmentations; usability scores (reported by group comparisons)
metrics_performance: classification accuracy; standard-plane quality assessment vs clinicians; agreement rates; ability to discriminate expertise
main_findings: Prospective end-user validation reveals: high classification performance; clinician-level performance for plane quality; substantial agreement on explanations/segmentations; most clinicians find explanations/segmentations useful; AI confidence correlates with operator expertise, suggesting utility for competence assessment. Highlights that prospective evaluation surfaces challenges/opportunities not visible in retrospective validation.
conclusions: Prospective, multi-level clinical validation is essential for trustworthy XAI in fetal ultrasound; actionable concept-based explanations plus segmentation feedback can support clinician performance and may enable competence assessment, but future work should study interactive collaboration and clinical impact.
limitations: Did not assess impact on scan quality improvement or clinical outcomes; usability testing voluntary (possible selection bias); clinicians could not interact with or revise concept assumptions (limited interactivity/human-AI collaboration).

title: Navigating artificial general intelligence development: societal, technological, ethical, and brain-inspired pathways
domain: AGI; responsible AI; human-centered AI; trust and interpretability
user_group: policymakers; AI researchers; human factors community
task: systematic literature review and thematic mapping of AGI development pathways
model: PRISMA-guided Scopus retrieval; BERTopic topic modeling with transformer embeddings + UMAP + HDBSCAN + c-TF-IDF; manual topic validation
explanation_techniques: PRISMA; BERTopic; UMAP; HDBSCAN; c-TF-IDF
evaluation_design: systematic review; Scopus search (titles/abstracts) run Oct 30, 2024; 1296 records retained; topic modeling with manual validation; narrative synthesis
participant_type: Scopus records (articles/reviews/conf papers/books/book chapters; 2003–2024; English)
main_findings: Five-theme roadmap highlights a dedicated pathway around explainability/trust/interpretability as a core AGI challenge; recommends interdisciplinary governance and human-centered integration to mitigate ethical/societal risks.
conclusions: AGI progress should be guided by research programs that explicitly integrate societal deployment, transparency/trust, and ethical accountability alongside technical capability gains.
limitations: Single-database reliance (Scopus) may omit relevant work from WoS/IEEE/arXiv; topic modeling depends on abstracts/metadata and may miss nuance; conceptual synthesis does not evaluate empirical AGI systems.

title: Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records
domain: EHR deep learning; clinical prediction; interpretability case study
task: build scalable deep learning models directly from raw EHR (FHIR) and evaluate clinical prediction performance
model: deep learning on sequential FHIR representation
explanation_techniques: case study interpretability: identifying relevant information from patient chart; discusses alert fatigue risks from false positives and clinical importance of usable predictions
evaluation_design: retrospective multi-site validation study using de-identified EHR data
participant_type: hospitalized adult patients (two US centers)
metrics_understanding: understanding via highlighting relevant chart information (case study)
metrics_performance: AUROC across tasks and sites; comparison vs traditional models
main_findings: Shows raw-EHR deep learning is scalable and accurate across sites; argues leveraging full record (including notes) reduces manual feature engineering; highlights clinical risks of false alarms (alert fatigue) and motivates interpretability to identify relevant information from charts as part of trustworthy deployment.
conclusions: Concludes FHIR-based sequential representations enable accurate, scalable clinical predictions across centers and may support a variety of clinical scenarios; interpretability mechanisms can surface relevant chart information in specific predictions.
limitations: Retrospective validation; deployment impact and calibration in real workflows not assessed; interpretability details are case-study level and may not guarantee explanation faithfulness.

title: Diagnostic accuracy of deep learning in medical imaging: a systematic review and meta-analysis
domain: medical imaging deep learning; evaluation standards; reporting quality
task: synthesize diagnostic accuracy and methodological quality of DL imaging studies
model: deep learning diagnostic models (mostly CNNs)
data_modality: medical images (retinal fundus/OCT; chest X-ray/CT; mammogram/US/MRI/DBT)
explanation_techniques: primarily performance evaluation (AUC) and reporting standards; emphasizes need for AI-specific reporting guidelines to reduce bias/overestimation
evaluation_design: systematic review + random-effects meta-analysis
metrics_performance: AUC ranges and heterogeneity; critique of design/reporting variability
main_findings: Finds high pooled diagnostic performance across several domains (e.g., ophthalmology AUC ~0.93–1; respiratory ~0.86–0.94; breast ~0.87–0.91) but warns heterogeneity and inconsistent methods/terminology/outcomes can inflate reported accuracy and limit generalizability; highlights urgent need for AI-specific reporting standards.
conclusions: Concludes that despite promising diagnostic accuracy, reporting and methodological variation threatens reliability of evidence; recommends developing AI-specific EQUATOR/STARD guidance to improve study quality and appraisal.
limitations: Limited to studies up to Jan 2020; publication bias and heterogeneity; results depend on study quality and inconsistent ground truth/reference standards across included papers.

title: Explainable medical imaging AI needs human-centered design: guidelines and evidence from a systematic review
domain: human-centered XAI for medical imaging; transparency as affordance
task: systematically review transparent ML in medical image analysis and propose design guideline
model: INTRPRT guideline for transparent ML design (review)
explanation_techniques: transparent ML / XAI as affordance; calls for formative user research, rapid prototyping, and iterative empirical user studies; introduces INTRPRT guideline (design directive)
evaluation_design: systematic review + guideline proposal
metrics_trust: trustworthiness/confidence/fairness/interactivity mentioned as transparency-related goals; emphasizes risk of clinical irrelevance without user validation
metrics_reliance: misuse/disuse risk and belief-updating issues implied by lack of usable transparency
metrics_understanding: user understanding via validated transparency affordances and prototypes
main_findings: Finds almost no studies report formative user research; only few validate transparency claims with empirical user evaluations; warns that without human-centered design, “transparent” ML may be incomprehensible and clinically irrelevant; proposes INTRPRT to ground design choices in user/context understanding and to mitigate misspent effort.
conclusions: Concludes that transparent ML in medical imaging needs human-centered design; recommends formative user research first, then iterative prototyping and empirical validation so that transparency affordances match stakeholder needs.
limitations: Review limited to selected databases/time window (2012–2021); access/ethics and knowledge imbalance hinder user studies; guideline is prescriptive and requires future evaluation in practice.

title: Solving the explainable AI conundrum by bridging clinicians’ needs and developers’ goals
domain: neurocritical care; clinical decision support; human-centered XAI
task: co-design XAI for delayed cerebral ischemia prediction (DCIP) and characterize clinician vs developer mental models/requirements
model: ML voting model combining static and dynamic Extremely Randomized Trees pipelines (AUC ~0.747)
data_modality: EHR + lab time series + clinical variables
explanation_techniques: Shapley/SHAP feature contributions (found unhelpful for clinicians); recommends causal inference + counterfactual/what-if querying; personalized explanations; cohort-level evidence
interaction_design: interactive high-fidelity UI prototype with risk timeline and feature contributions; hover/probe interactions; integration with EHR context
evaluation_design: longitudinal multi-method co-design (survey + focus group + 11 interviews); scenario-based questions; hierarchical regression; qualitative thematic analysis
participant_type: clinicians (N-ICU) and ML developers
metrics_trust: intention/willingness to use; trust tied to clinical plausibility; effort expectancy
metrics_reliance: risk assessment with/without DCIP (scenario-based); intention-to-use predicted by performance/effort expectancy
metrics_understanding: perceived usefulness of explanations; mismatch between “interpretability” and clinician sense-making
metrics_performance: model AUC (voting model ~0.747 ± 0.094; dynamic ~0.729; static ~0.679)
main_findings: Finds three recurring clinician–developer gaps: (1) goals differ (developers seek model interpretability to assess reliability/bias; clinicians seek clinical plausibility), (2) sources of truth differ (data vs patient/context), and (3) mindsets differ (exploration vs exploitation). Reports clinicians found standard post-hoc attributions (e.g., Shapley values) insufficient for establishing trust in time-critical use; proposes design directions emphasizing causal reasoning, personalization, interactive probing, and balancing exploration/exploitation (“ambidexterity”).
conclusions: Argues successful healthcare XAI requires bridging clinician and developer mental models; recommends combining interpretability with clinical plausibility, increased interactivity, and causal ML support for hypotheticals while matching the time-critical, high-risk clinical workflow.
limitations: Relies on a single hospital and a single ML system; external sampling limited during COVID-19; correlation-trained model means counterfactual probing can mislead users into causal interpretations.

title: Eye tracking insights into physician behaviour with safe and unsafe explainable AI recommendations
domain: human-AI interaction; automation bias; XAI evaluation; ICU decision support
task: test whether XAI draws attention and helps reject unsafe AI recommendations
model: AI decision support with 4 XAI types
explanation_techniques: four simultaneously presented XAI types (categories not detailed in abstract excerpt); evaluates via objective eye-tracking and subjective usefulness ratings; highlights that self-report-only evaluation misses interaction behavior
interaction_design: AI recommendation revealed after initial decision (human-first then AI reveal) in simulation; explanations shown alongside recommendation
evaluation_design: controlled clinical simulation study with eye-tracking instrumentation
participant_type: ICU physicians
metrics_trust: trust/reliance inferred via influence-of-AI and behavior; warns about automation bias
metrics_reliance: no evidence XAI “rescues” decision-makers from unsafe advice
metrics_understanding: attention to explanations (fixations) and self-reported usefulness compared
metrics_performance: decision changes pre/post advice; influence-of-AI metrics reported
main_findings: Finds unsafe AI advice increases attention to recommendation itself, but explanations do not receive additional attention in unsafe scenarios; self-reported usefulness fails to track objective attention; suggests that evaluating XAI must include behavioral measures and that XAI may not prevent unsafe reliance in high-stakes settings.
conclusions: Concludes eye-tracking can reveal hidden aspects of clinician-AI interaction; XAI may not automatically mitigate unsafe recommendation risks; recommends going beyond self-report for XAI evaluation.
limitations: Simulation setting limits ecological validity; explanations shown simultaneously may affect attention allocation; sample size modest and real-hospital standardization challenges remain.

title: Explainable AI reveals Clever Hans effects in unsupervised learning models
domain: unsupervised learning; representation learning; anomaly detection; spurious correlations
user_group: ML researchers and practitioners using foundation/unsupervised models
task: detect and analyze Clever Hans effects in unsupervised models
model: Explainable analysis based on layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) and BiLRP applied to unsupervised representation models (PubMedCLIP/CLIP/SimCLR/Barlow Twins) and anomaly detection models (e.g., D2Neighbors)
explanation_techniques: LRP; BiLRP
evaluation_design: evaluates unsupervised representations via downstream classification tasks and subgroup stress tests (e.g., COVID-19 NIH vs GitHub; ImageNet trucks with logos; fish with humans) and reports accuracy/FPR changes; analyzes anomaly detection on MVTec-AD
participant_type: NIH CXR8 images (2597) + GitHub COVID images (535) for COVID analysis; also ImageNet and MVTec-AD for other experiments
metrics_performance: PubMedCLIP COVID accuracy 87.5% overall; GitHub subgroup accuracy 81.7% with FPR 51% (CH-related failure under heterogeneity)
main_findings: Unsupervised models can rely on spurious cues (e.g., text/logos/humans) that do not generalize; BiLRP reveals such cues without specifying a downstream task and helps pinpoint causes and mitigation strategies.
conclusions: Robust deployment of unsupervised/foundation models needs systematic XAI-based auditing for CH effects and robustness checks under dataset shifts.
limitations: Findings depend on selected datasets/tasks and explanation assumptions; explanations are correlational; mitigation may not remove all spurious dependencies across domains.

title: Explainable AI decision support improves accuracy during telehealth strep throat screening
domain: telehealth; clinical decision support; XAI for clinician trust and intelligibility
user_group: US primary care telehealth providers (physicians, nurse practitioners, physician assistants)
task: test how explainable AI interface elements affect clinician accuracy, agreement, trust, and understanding vs standard Centor Score
model: AI-based CDSS for strep throat from smartphone throat images (prototype outputs) + baseline Modified Centor Score; XAI elements: performance characteristics, feature contribution, example-based
explanation_techniques: XAI-1: performance characteristics (sensitivity/specificity/PPV/NPV) disclosure; XAI-2: Explain by Feature Contribution (post-hoc feature importance over visual signs; Shapley-style); XAI-3: Explain by Examples (similar/dissimilar cases)
interaction_design: randomized three-arm between-subjects design after within-subject baseline; participants complete 8 cases (4 Centor, then 4 AI with assigned XAI element) and answer diagnosis + follow-up action questions plus trust/understanding items
evaluation_design: web-based REDCap user study; compares conditions on sensitivity/specificity/PPV/NPV, agreement with CDSS, confirmatory testing behavior, perceived trust and understanding using linear/mixed models
participant_type: telehealth providers in US urgent/primary care (valid participants; recruited 295, excluded to 121)
metrics_trust: perceived trust: 3 statements, 7-point Likert (Cronbach’s α=0.95); indirect trust: fraction of negative cases recommending confirmatory test
