metrics_reliance: agreement rate with CDSS prediction; follow-up action choices (supportive care/antibiotics/test)
metrics_understanding: subjective understanding: 3 statements, 7-point Likert (Cronbach’s α=0.79)
metrics_performance: sensitivity/specificity; PPV/NPV; task accuracy proxies
main_findings: AI-based CDSS increased diagnostic performance (improved prediction vs Centor) and clinician agreement with advice, but decreased perceived trust relative to Centor and increased confirmatory test recommendations; different explanation elements induced tradeoffs, with example-based support affecting trust and feature-based support affecting understanding differently.
conclusions: Even when AI improves remote screening accuracy, human–AI collaboration barriers remain: clinicians may not trust or understand AI advice, leading to conservative actions (more confirmatory testing). Designing XAI for telehealth CDSS requires balancing transparency, understandability, and trust calibration rather than assuming explanations automatically increase trust.
limitations: Limited cases per participant (4 per condition, only 2 positives); early-stage prototype with manually defined AI outputs; limited ability to study intention-to-use; limited samples per participant and group variability may affect model estimates; explanation/performance indicator choices constrained by study complexity and predefined subset.

title: Exploring Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Transparent Decision Making
domain: XAI overview; transparency; trust; decision support
task: overview/survey of XAI methods for transparent decisions
model: survey-style discussion of interpretable models and explanation methods; includes UI design considerations and workflow steps for building explainable systems
explanation_techniques: General XAI (interpretable models; model-agnostic explanations; visualization)
interaction_design: UI/visualization guidance for presenting explanations to users
evaluation_design: survey/position paper; no empirical evaluation
main_findings: Emphasizes that transparency and explanation presentation (including UI) are key drivers of stakeholder trust and adoption; highlights risks of opaque models in high-stakes domains.
conclusions: Adoption-oriented XAI work benefits from combining interpretable modeling with explanation interfaces and governance for responsible decision making.
limitations: Lacks empirical validation and concrete evaluation metrics; broad treatment may oversimplify method trade-offs and domain constraints.

title: Trust in AI and Its Role in the Acceptance of AI Technologies
domain: trust in AI; technology acceptance; multidimensional trust measurement
user_group: general consumers; users of voice assistants/smart technologies
task: explain and test the role of trust in intention to use AI technologies using an extended Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
model: extended TAM path models; Study 2 develops 2-factor trust scale via exploratory factor analysis (human-like trust vs functionality trust)
data_modality: online surveys (attitudes and intentions about AI voice assistants/smart technologies)
explanation_techniques: trust measurement and dimensionality (human-like vs functionality); relates trust to perceived usefulness/ease-of-use/attitude; discusses explainability as contributor to perceived trustworthiness
evaluation_design: two survey studies with path analysis (lavaan) and EFA (Oblimin rotation) for trust items; examines indirect effects via usefulness and attitude
participant_type: college students (Study 1; N=312) + US adults (Study 2; Qualtrics panel; N=640)
metrics_trust: trust perception scale (Study 1: 4 items; Study 2: 11 items yielding human-like trust (6) and functionality trust (5)); trust treated as TAM construct affecting usefulness and attitude
metrics_reliance: intention to use/continue using AI technologies; acceptance modeled via TAM constructs
main_findings: Trust is a significant predictor of AI acceptance in extended TAM, largely via indirect pathways through perceived usefulness and attitude; trust in AI is multidimensional, and functionality-related trust tends to exert a stronger total influence on usage intention than human-like trust.
conclusions: Extending TAM with multidimensional trust improves explanation/prediction of AI technology acceptance and provides a reusable trust measurement approach for trustworthy AI research and design interventions.
limitations: Focuses on relatively low-risk consumer AI (voice assistants/smart tech), limiting generalization to high-stakes AI; trust is dynamic (initial vs ongoing) but measured cross-sectionally; path models are correlational (not causal); online panels may not fully represent the population (tech-access bias).

title: Towards interpreting machine learning models for predicting soil moisture droughts
domain: soil moisture drought prediction; explainable AI; ecohydrology
task: site-specific soil moisture drought prediction and event-level explanation
model: Random Forest site-specific models; global interpretation via permutation importance and accumulated local effects; local interpretation via SHAP, LIME, and ICE
data_modality: FLUXNET site meteorology/flux variables and soil moisture
explanation_techniques: Permutation importance; ALE; SHAP; LIME; ICE
evaluation_design: site-specific modeling across 30 sites; compares global vs local explanations and cross-validates insights across multiple XAI methods; analyzes 504 drought events
participant_type: 30 FLUXNET sites (8 vegetation types); 504 drought events
main_findings: Local interpretations suggest stronger reliance on soil temperature, atmospheric aridity, and latent heat flux at grass sites, while forest sites show higher correlation to time-dependent parameters; using multiple XAI tools enables mutual verification and can reveal physically inconsistent model behavior.
conclusions: Combining multiple XAI methods supports more physically grounded understanding of ML-based soil moisture drought predictions and may increase scientific trust and adoption.
limitations: Causal mechanisms remain uncertain; correlated features can destabilize local explanations; general laws of SM drought variation remain unclear and broader validation is needed.

title: CX-Net: an efficient ensemble semantic deep neural network for ROI identification from chest-x-ray images for COPD diagnosis
domain: COPD diagnosis; lung segmentation; CXR; explainable AI
task: identify lung ROI / segment lungs in chest X-rays for downstream COPD diagnosis
model: CX-Net ensemble of FPN, U-Net, LinkNet, and customized U-Net with ImageNet encoder plus preprocessing/augmentation and outlier resistance
explanation_techniques: SHAP; Grad-CAM
evaluation_design: semantic segmentation evaluation on Montgomery (with masks) and generalization to VinDr-CXR; reports Jaccard/Dice/precision/recall/accuracy
participant_type: VinDr-CXR annotated images (18,000) plus Montgomery CXR set; VinDr contains >100,000 CXRs overall
metrics_performance: Jaccard 0.992; Dice 0.994; Precision 0.993; Recall 0.980; Accuracy 0.976
main_findings: Ensembling improves lung ROI extraction robustness; explanations via SHAP/Grad-CAM provide clinician-facing transparency about salient regions in CXRs.
conclusions: Combining high-performing segmentation ensembles with visual explanations can strengthen clinical trust and support integration into CDSS pipelines.
limitations: Focuses on PA CXR segmentation and excludes lateral images; AP CXR handling is not addressed; performance depends on dataset labeling and preprocessing; explanation methods remain correlational.

title: Interpretable machine learning model to predict survival days of malignant brain tumor patients
domain: glioblastoma; radiomics; survival prediction; interpretability
task: predict survival days (SD) for GBM patients from MRI-derived features
model: end-to-end pipeline: brain tumor segmentation (No-New-Net) + radiomics/metadata feature extraction + feature selection + Random Forest Regression; interpretability via SHAP, PDP, and Kaplan-Meier
explanation_techniques: SHAP; PDP; Kaplan-Meier analysis
evaluation_design: BraTS-2020 evaluation; five-fold cross-validation for SD model; uses multiple metrics (accuracy, MSE, medianSE, stdSE, Spearman) and explanation analyses
participant_type: BraTS-2020 training set: 369 MRI samples; SD metadata available for 236 patients; validation set: 125 samples with 29 SD metadata
metrics_performance: Reports ~33.33% accuracy improvement over BraTS-2020 top methods; uses MSE/medianSE/stdSE/Spearman
main_findings: Age and selected radiomic and location-based features dominate SD predictions; interpretability analyses align with clinical intuition (survival decreases after ~60 and is scarce beyond ~80).
conclusions: Interpretable radiomics+metadata models can improve GBM survival prediction by pairing predictive performance with feature-level explanations that support clinical understanding.
limitations: Generalization depends on BraTS cohort and segmentation quality; SD metadata is incomplete for the full training set; interpretability remains correlational and requires external clinical validation.

title: Assessing decadal variability of subseasonal forecasts of opportunity using explainable AI
domain: subseasonal predictability; forecasts of opportunity; explainable neural networks
task: subseasonal precipitation prediction and identification of forecasts of opportunity
model: explainable neural networks trained on CESM2-LE and applied to ERA5; uses confidence and XAI heatmaps to identify key regions/signals
data_modality: gridded climate model and reanalysis precipitation fields
explanation_techniques: XAI heatmaps; confidence-based interpretability
evaluation_design: trains on CESM2 (1850–1949) and evaluates on additional CESM2 members and ERA5; analyzes accuracy versus confidence and moving-window decadal variability
participant_type: CESM2-LE climate model data (1000 years; ten members sampled) and ERA5 reanalysis (1959–2021)
metrics_performance: Forecast-of-opportunity accuracy reported >65% in ERA5 for some periods; higher accuracy at higher confidence
main_findings: Forecasts of opportunity vary on decadal timescales; confidence and heatmaps help diagnose when tropically driven predictability transfers to extratropical precipitation skill.
conclusions: Explainable neural networks can identify predictable climate states and support more reliable subseasonal forecasts by coupling skill estimates with interpretable drivers.
limitations: Skill varies by region/decade and can drop to near-random; interpretability is correlational; transfer from model-trained relationships to reanalysis depends on model fidelity and non-stationarity.

title: shap-ing the gas: understanding gas shapes in dark matter haloes with interpretable machine learning
venue: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
domain: astrophysics; dark matter haloes; gas shape modelling; interpretability
task: predict gas shape radial profiles from halo properties
model: XGBoost gradient boosted trees to predict gas shape profiles; interpret feature importance across radii with SHAP
data_modality: using IllustrisTNG simulations (TNG100 and TNG300 halo properties)
explanation_techniques: SHAP
evaluation_design: trains and evaluates XGBoost models on TNG halo samples; interprets feature rankings by radius and mass bin; reports model performance via RMSE and distributional comparisons
participant_type: TNG halo sample (TNG300 population noted as 2,548 haloes; includes TNG100 for lower masses)
metrics_performance: Model performance reported via RMSE (including ~30% improvement in outer radial bins when including dark matter shapes, per appendix discussion)
main_findings: SHAP reveals radius-dependent physical drivers: baryonic effects are most predictive in cores while dark matter shapes and halo mass dominate at larger radii; interpretable ML helps connect predictors to halo physics.
conclusions: Interpretable boosted-tree models can forward-model gas shape systematics for cluster cosmology while providing insight into the physical processes shaping gas morphology.
limitations: Models are limited by simulation realism and sampled halo populations; extrapolation to haloes not represented in training simulations is uncertain; SHAP explanations are associational and not causal.

title: Explainable Intrusion Detection Systems (X-IDS): A Survey of Current Methods, Challenges, and Opportunities
domain: explainable IDS; cybersecurity; human-in-the-loop; stakeholder-centered explanations
user_group: CSoC analysts, IDS developers, and organizational stakeholders (survey focus)
task: survey XAI approaches for intrusion detection; propose taxonomy and human-in-the-loop architecture; identify evaluation challenges
model: X-IDS approaches: white-box (interpretable-by-design) and black-box with post-hoc explainers (e.g., LIME/SHAP/Anchors/LRP)
data_modality: network flow and security event data (discussed across literature; no new dataset)
explanation_techniques: model-agnostic and model-specific explainers discussed (LIME, Anchors, SHAP, DeepLIFT, LRP); local vs global explainability; faithfulness and related metrics; stakeholder-oriented explanation design
evaluation_design: survey paper; synthesizes prior X-IDS work; proposes taxonomy and architecture; outlines challenges and recommendations
metrics_trust: trust-building as stated goal (qualitative; recommendations)
metrics_understanding: understandability/explainability definitions; stakeholder-tailored explanation needs
main_findings: X-IDS must support CSoC decision-making by providing reasoning for detections/misclassifications; survey highlights that opaque models can be accurate but unusable without explanations. Proposes taxonomy separating white-box vs black-box+post-hoc methods and argues why black-box+post-hoc is often used for IDS performance, but needs rigorous stakeholder-centered evaluation and metrics.
conclusions: Calls for clearer, domain-specific definitions of explainability for IDS, stakeholder-specific explanation requirements (developers vs CSoC analysts vs others), and standardized metrics to evaluate explanations to enable trustworthy adoption in operational security contexts.
limitations: Survey-based synthesis; heterogeneity of IDS data/settings and evaluation; lack of consensus metrics for explanations; notes potential for adversarial manipulation and data quality issues impacting explanations.

title: explAIner: A Visual Analytics Framework for Interactive and Explainable Machine Learning
venue: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics (2019)
domain: interactive XAI; visual analytics; provenance and trust-building in ML workflows
user_group: model novices; model users; model developers
task: enable interactive model understanding, diagnosis, refinement, and reporting/trust building in a unified visual analytics workflow
model: explAIner visual analytics system built on TensorFlow/TensorBoard; integrates multiple explainers (e.g., LIME, Anchors, LRP, Saliency, DeepTaylor) and provenance tracking
explanation_techniques: integrates multiple explanation methods (local/global; model/data/domain-dependent) including LIME, Anchors, LRP variants, saliency and others; provenance tracking and comparative reporting to support justification/trust building
interaction_design: interactive visual analytics workflow with phases (understand, diagnose, refine) and global monitoring/steering; pair-analytics think-aloud sessions; reporting/export for sharing
evaluation_design: results from qualitative pair-analytics user study (9 ~1-hour sessions): semi-structured interview + guided tasks + free exploration; captures user feedback across experience levels
participant_type: 2 model novices (CS PhD; basic ML; no NN), 2 model users (linguistic data analysis; no DL), 5 model developers (2 industry devs, 3 students; TensorFlow/TensorBoard)
metrics_trust: n/a (trust building treated as workflow goal; qualitative feedback on “gain trust in models”)
metrics_understanding: user feedback on understanding/diagnosis/refinement usability and guidance needs
main_findings: Participants broadly agreed with the pipeline and valued diagnosis/refinement capabilities; provenance tracking/reporting was consistently liked for communicating results and supporting justification. Users requested more guidance/tutorials, simplified or alternative model representations beyond TensorBoard graphs, more explainers, and stronger data sharing across workflow tabs; the TensorBoard plugin architecture imposed practical constraints for integrated workflows.
conclusions: A unified interactive+explainable workflow benefits ML development and evaluation by combining explanation methods with provenance and reporting; practical adoption requires user-adapted guidance and architectures that support seamless data/model/explanation sharing across stages.
limitations: Small qualitative study with limited participant diversity (only one female); using TensorBoard as a base introduced UI and data-sharing limitations due to plugin separation; some requested features (e.g., on-demand refinement) not straightforward within current architecture; system complexity may challenge beginners without additional guidance.

title: A Survey on Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): Toward Medical XAI
venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems (survey)
domain: medical XAI; interpretability frameworks; accountability/reliability
user_group: clinicians; medical AI practitioners; XAI researchers
task: survey interpretability methods and organize them for medical contexts
model: n/a (survey of XAI methods)
explanation_techniques: categorization of interpretability types (e.g., perceptive interpretability including saliency/heatmaps and LIME-like highlighting; signal-based/mathematical structure methods); discusses interpretability assessment criteria (reliability, causality, usability) and the lack of standard adoption
evaluation_design: survey with proposed taxonomy/categorization
metrics_trust: trustworthiness/reliability as motivations; accountability when things go wrong emphasized
metrics_reliance: clinician caution and appropriate use emphasized
main_findings: Provides a categorization intended to help practitioners navigate diverse XAI methods and interpretability criteria; emphasizes that lack of uniform interpretability assessment and black-box DL risks necessitate clearer frameworks, especially for medical use; encourages mathematically grounded and technically grounded medical education around AI.
conclusions: Concludes that organizing interpretability methods and assessment criteria can guide safer medical adoption and stimulate better alignment between XAI research and medical practice needs.
limitations: Survey perspective and categorization choices may be subjective; does not resolve standardization across communities; rapid evolution of XAI methods may date parts of the taxonomy.

title: Why Should I Trust You?: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier
domain: model-agnostic explanation; trust calibration
task: justify and inspect individual predictions; select/deploy models; debug and improve models
model: LIME local surrogate explanations; SP-LIME (submodular pick of representative explanations)
explanation_techniques: LIME (local interpretable model-agnostic explanations); interpretable representations (words/superpixels); local fidelity-interpretability trade-off; SP-LIME (submodular pick) for global model understanding
interaction_design: UI patterns for explanations: highlighted words with weights; superpixel-based image explanations; show side-by-side explanations to compare models
evaluation_design: Simulated trustworthiness evaluation + human-subject studies: (i) users choose better-generalizing model via explanations, (ii) users do feature engineering by removing untrustworthy features from explanations, (iii) users detect spurious correlations in vision (husky vs wolf) from explanations
participant_type: MTurk adults (religion familiarity) + iterative feature-engineering chains; graduate students (ML) for vision spurious-correlation insight
metrics_trust: task success selecting better classifier; self-reported trust in bad model drops after seeing explanations (10/27 → 3/27)
metrics_reliance: calibrated reliance via identifying untrustworthy features and preferring cleaned model; feature-removal to improve generalization
metrics_understanding: identifies spurious correlations (e.g., snow background in wolf/husky) from explanations and describes model irregularities
metrics_performance: real-world accuracy/generalization proxy via user model-choice and via retrained models after deleting features
main_findings: Shows that local explanations help users assess when not to trust predictions, choose between models with similar validation accuracy, and identify spurious correlations/data issues; SP-LIME improves selection by presenting diverse, representative explanations; explanations can enable non-experts to improve an untrustworthy model via feature removal.
conclusions: Concludes that trust is crucial for effective human–ML interaction; proposes LIME for faithful, interpretable, model-agnostic explanations and SP-LIME for representative global understanding; experiments support usefulness across text/image trust tasks for expert and non-expert users.
limitations: Limited treatment of pick step for images (not addressed in this work); additional explanation families (e.g., trees) need user comparisons; needs theoretical guidance (sampling) and computational optimizations for real-time, human-in-the-loop use.

title: Explaining Machine Learning Classifiers through Diverse Counterfactual Explanations
domain: counterfactual explanations; actionability/diversity
user_group: end users + other stakeholders (model designers, decision-makers, auditors)
task: generate a diverse feasible set of counterfactual explanations for a given prediction
model: DiCE / DiverseCF (DPP-based diverse counterfactual generation)
explanation_techniques: counterfactual explanations; DPP-based diversity objective; proximity metrics; optional causal feasibility filtering; evaluation via local boundary approximation
interaction_design: notes need for interfaces to select constraints/scales; no implemented interactive UI
evaluation_design: experimental evaluation on 4 real-world tabular datasets; compares to baselines and to LIME using precision/recall/F1 of local boundary classifiers; analyzes feasibility via causal constraints (post-hoc)
metrics_performance: precision/recall/F1 of local boundary approximation
main_findings: Finds DiverseCF can generate many unique valid and diverse counterfactuals and often approximates local decision boundaries at least as well as LIME; highlights feasibility issues from naive independent feature changes and motivates incorporating causal constraints during generation.
conclusions: Concludes diverse and feasible counterfactual sets are useful explanations; identifies future work on black-box settings (no gradients), causal constraints during generation (not only post-hoc), behavioral studies, and tradeoffs with “choice overload” as k grows.
limitations: Assumes access to model gradients; feasibility constraints are handled post-hoc (in this work) and depend on domain knowledge; no user study; distances/costs may not reflect human effort or policy constraints in practice.

title: How AI Developers Overcome Communication Challenges in a Multidisciplinary Team: A Case Study
venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction (PACMHCI / CSCW)
domain: HCAI development; multidisciplinary AI teams; shared mental models; communication; trust
user_group: AI developers (data scientists and AI-adjacent roles); external stakeholders (product/client teams)
task: understand and document inter-role communication gaps and strategies in AI projects; analyze explanatory artifacts used across boundaries
model: n/a (qualitative case study; analyzes communication practices and artifacts rather than proposing a new AI algorithm)
data_modality: semi-structured interviews; artifact analysis (e.g., slide decks)
explanation_techniques: explanatory communication artifacts (e.g., slide decks, visualizations); shared mental model principles for structuring explanations and managing expectations
interaction_design: remote interview sessions; iterative artifact review; emphasizes tailoring explanations to stakeholder roles and project stages
evaluation_design: qualitative: 10 interview sessions across 4 participants; project-overview phase + artifact-in-depth phase; triangulation with artifact analysis; thematic coding with shared mental models lens
participant_type: AI practitioners (IBM internal AI team; multiple roles across teams)
metrics_trust: trust and expectation management themes in communication (qualitative)
metrics_understanding: shared mental models / alignment of task understanding (qualitative)
main_findings: AI developers face recurring gaps due to knowledge mismatch; they use proactive, reactive, and educational communication strategies and rely heavily on flexible, familiar artifacts (often PowerPoint/slide decks) to tailor explanations and align expectations across roles. Findings highlight that “one size does not fit all” for explanations; artifacts are iteratively customized and reused informally.
conclusions: Improving human-centered AI development requires tools and practices that support role-tailored explanations, expectation setting, and shared mental models across multidisciplinary boundaries; artifact-centered communication is central but currently costly and hard to maintain/search.
limitations: Small single-company sample (IBM) and only four participants; confidentiality limits artifact sharing; formative results not broadly generalizable; only AI-developer perspective (limited stakeholder-side data).

title: To Trust or to Think: Cognitive Forcing Functions Can Reduce Overreliance on AI in AI-assisted Decision-making
domain: AI-assisted decision-making; overreliance/overtrust; calibrated trust; debiasing via interaction design
task: test whether cognitive forcing interaction designs reduce overreliance on AI compared to simple explainable AI baselines
model: simulated AI decision support in a meal-ingredient replacement task; provides top substitutions + feature-based explanations; includes confidence prompt in uncertainty baseline
data_modality: images of meals + tabular ingredient options
explanation_techniques: feature-based explanations for recommended substitutions (estimated carbohydrate reduction + flavor similarity); confidence prompt for uncertainty; cognitive forcing functions as debiasing interventions (on-demand access to AI suggestion, forced initial decision then update, and waiting period before seeing AI output)
interaction_design: mixed within/between design with 6 conditions (no AI; explanation; uncertainty; on-demand; update; wait); participants choose ingredient to replace and replacement; per-block subjective questionnaires
evaluation_design: experiment on MTurk (US adults); objective performance and overreliance metrics plus subjective measures; analyzes effects by category (no AI, simple XAI, cognitive forcing) and by Need for Cognition subgroup
participant_type: MTurk adults residing in US (260 recruited; exclusions yield 199 analyzed)
metrics_trust: trust rating: “I trust this AI’s suggestions for optimal replacement.” (5-point Likert)
metrics_reliance: overreliance: agreement with AI when AI is incorrect; human error on incorrect AI predictions
metrics_understanding: n/a (focus is overreliance and performance; collects mental demand and system complexity self-reports)
metrics_performance: overall performance; carb source detection; carb reduction; flavor similarity; sensitivity to incorrect AI suggestions
main_findings: Cognitive forcing designs reduced overreliance compared to simple explainable AI baselines, improving ability to detect AI errors, but at the cost of lower subjective acceptance/usability. Lower trust and preference were associated with better detection of incorrect AI. Benefits were stronger for participants with higher Need for Cognition, raising concerns about intervention-generated inequalities.
conclusions: Reducing overreliance requires not only providing explanations but also designing interactions that trigger analytical thinking at decision time; however, these interventions must be balanced against usability and potential differential benefits across users.
limitations: Single non-critical task; cognitive forcing improves performance but reduces perceived usability (may reduce adoption); effects may vary across domains/settings; may exacerbate inequalities by benefiting high-NFC users more; motivates adaptive deployment based on uncertainty and user differences.

Consistently discusses groundedness/validation per method and highlights lack of standardization in interpretability benchmarks; encourages work on sequence-to-sequence explanation and bridging intrinsic/post-hoc middle grounds.

title: Ethical use of artificial intelligence to prevent sudden cardiac death: an interview study of patient perspectives

explanation_techniques: trustworthy AI requirements as discussion framework (human agency/oversight, transparency, accountability, etc.); focuses on explainability and patient trust; emphasizes human-touch as clinically relevant factor
interaction_design: interview scenarios with graded AI role; participants classify ethics-requirement labels per scenario
evaluation_design: qualitative interview study with thematic analysis
participant_type: patients with ICD and/or high-risk heart condition
metrics_trust: patient trust and trustworthiness perceptions (qualitative themes)
metrics_understanding: patient understanding of AI role and explanations (qualitative)
main_findings: Finds six themes: AI may rectify human limitations and provide objectivity/second opinion, but patients fear loss of “human touch” and want doctors to evaluate AI recommendations in context with patient values; “human touch” extends beyond kindness and has clinical relevance; calls for patient-centered AI integration beyond principle lists.
conclusions: Concludes normative research on a “right to a human doctor” may be needed and policies should address ethics of everyday practice; recommends empirical ethics grounded in ethnography to guide patient-centered AI integration.
limitations: Qualitative and context-specific (ICD/SCD); scenario-based perceptions may differ from real deployment experiences; cross-country sample size modest.

title: Use of AI-based tools for healthcare purposes: a survey study from consumers’ perspectives
venue: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
domain: consumer adoption of AI-based CDS; perceived benefits/risks; trust and regulation
task: examine perceived benefits/risks and intention to use AI medical devices with CDS features
model: AI-based medical devices with CDS features (consumer perspective)
explanation_techniques: considers transparency/interpretability as part of technological concerns; ethical concerns include mistrust mechanisms, privacy, and social bias; regulatory concerns include unregulated standards and liability; no specific XAI algorithm evaluated
evaluation_design: online survey study + model testing (consumer value perception framework)
participant_type: US adults (online survey)
metrics_trust: trust-related ethical concerns contribute to perceived risk
metrics_reliance: perceived risk influences adoption intention
metrics_understanding: understanding/communication barriers as a key concern
metrics_performance: intention to use (adoption) as outcome
main_findings: Shows perceived risks are driven by technological (performance anxiety, communication barriers), ethical, and regulatory concerns; recommends normative standards, evaluation guidelines, audits, and monitoring to improve safety, quality, transparency, and ethical factors for AI-based CDS services.
conclusions: Concludes addressing consumer risk perceptions is critical for AI-CDS adoption and calls for regulatory agencies and healthcare institutions to establish standards and continuous monitoring to reduce concerns and improve transparency/ethics.
limitations: Survey self-report and US-only sample; focuses on perceptions rather than observed behavior; does not evaluate real AI systems or explanation interventions.

title: A qualitative research framework for the design of user-centered displays of explanations for machine learning model predictions in healthcare
venue: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
domain: user-centered XAI in healthcare; explanation display design
task: design and evaluate user-centered explanation displays for an ML prediction tool
model: PICU in-hospital mortality risk model + instance-level feature influence explanations
explanation_techniques: instance-level feature influence via Shapley values; framework for explanation display design and context-of-use considerations
interaction_design: explanation display prototypes (including low-effort summaries and supporting contextual information) refined through provider feedback
evaluation_design: qualitative focus group study + iterative design review
participant_type: critical care providers (nurses, physicians, trainees)
metrics_trust: provider acceptance and trust/uptake concerns (qualitative)
metrics_understanding: provider understanding/interpretation needs (qualitative)
main_findings: Providers perceive explanation displays as useful but differ in goals and required detail; preferences favor reduced cognitive load and actionable/contextual information; supporting information is critical for understanding and acceptance; resulting design integrates provider-suggested enhancements into a user-centered explanation display for the PICU model.
conclusions: Concludes that user-centered, context-aware explanation display design is necessary for clinical ML adoption and that the proposed framework can guide development and evaluation of explanation interfaces for healthcare providers.
limitations: Limited setting and participant diversity; focus groups only and likely single-institution; does not measure downstream clinical outcomes; future work needed to generalize and to evaluate in real workflows.

title: Medically‑oriented design for explainable AI for stress prediction from physiological measurements
venue: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
domain: mental health; stress prediction; explainable report design
task: design an explainable report for stress prediction from wearable sensors
model: stress prediction model + explanation report generation
explanation_techniques: report-style explanations; Shapley values/SHAP-style feature attributions; reference intervals and ground-truth relationship checks
interaction_design: blood-test-like explanation report interface for clinicians/patients
evaluation_design: methods paper with quantitative validation + small expert qualitative survey
participant_type: expert psychiatrists
metrics_trust: trust/confidence via qualitative survey feedback
metrics_understanding: user understanding of biological feature contributions
main_findings: Finds explanations consistent with ground truth and distinctive reference intervals for stress vs non-stress; psychiatrists confirm usefulness and increased confidence/effectiveness of the report; proposes medically oriented, user-centric design to communicate XAI for mental health stress prediction.
conclusions: Concludes report-based explainable design helps identify impactful biological features and abnormalities; suggests extending to other emotional states (sadness, relaxation, anxiety, happiness).
limitations: Very small qualitative sample; evaluation focused on explanation consistency and expert perception rather than patient outcomes; generalizability depends on sensor data and stress ground truth.

title: Big data analytics: a survey
model: survey / literature review
evaluation_design: literature review; synthesis; no participants
conclusions: Conclusions not clearly detected in extracted text; check PDF tail.

title: Personality-based tailored explainable recommendation for trustworthy smart learning system in the age of artificial intelligence
domain: explainable recommender; personalization; Big Five; trustworthiness; K-12 math
task: test whether personality-tailored explanations increase engagement with explainable recommender
model: explainable quiz recommender with personality-based tailored messages; k-means clustering + A/B experiment
explanation_techniques: explainable recommendation (“why recommended” explanations) + tailored persuasive messages based on personality profiles
interaction_design: tailored messaging/intervention; explanation text variants based on quiz characteristics and personality profile
evaluation_design: A/B experiment (18 days) with control vs tailored explanation group
participant_type: high school students
metrics_performance: engagement/usage metrics (recommendation clicks, CVR)
main_findings: Engagement improves with personality-tailored explanations; suggests tailored explainable recommenders can support trustworthiness/acceptance in smart learning systems.
conclusions: Personality-based tailored explanations are more effective than quiz-characteristic-only explanations for e-learning engagement and likely improve trustworthiness of AI learning systems.
limitations: Insufficient sample size to compare matched vs unmatched explanations within profiles; Big Five inventory is long for K-12; concerns about inferring personality from logs and privacy when estimating personal information; clustering robustness needs verification.

title: The effects of over-reliance on AI dialogue systems on students' cognitive abilities: a systematic review
domain: generative AI; over-reliance; ethics; cognitive abilities; education
task: identify factors/ethical issues contributing to over-reliance on AI dialogue systems and impacts on cognition
model: systematic review following PRISMA; 14 articles across databases
explanation_techniques: n/a (not XAI method-focused; discusses transparency and reliability assessment)
evaluation_design: systematic review; no participants (synthesizes prior empirical studies)
participant_type: students (as population in reviewed studies)
metrics_trust: trust/reliability assessment challenges in over-reliance context
metrics_performance: cognitive ability impacts; ethics issues categories
main_findings: Highlights need for education strategies and safeguards to reduce uncritical acceptance of AI outputs and mitigate cognitive-skill erosion; frames over-reliance as trust calibration failure.
conclusions: Calls attention to ethical/behavioral pathways from generative AI use to over-reliance and cognitive impacts, motivating interventions and policy in educational AI deployment.
limitations: Limited to 14 retrieved studies and specific databases/time; heterogeneity of studies and constructs limits strong causal claims.

title: Why explainable AI may not be enough: predictions and mispredictions in decision making in education
domain: learning analytics; instance-level explainability; mispredictions; human-AI collaboration
task: evaluate instance-level explanations for student performance prediction and analyze mispredictions
model: ML prediction (5 algorithms; best RF) + explanatory mechanisms; compares aggregate vs instance-level; analyzes residuals/mispredictions
explanation_techniques: instance-level explanations (local explainability) for ML predictions; error/misprediction explanation emphasis
interaction_design: human involvement in interpretation/decision-making stressed; explanations intended to support teacher/student decisions
evaluation_design: experimental modeling + residual analysis; case analysis of mispredictions
participant_type: students
metrics_reliance: decision support usefulness discussed
metrics_performance: model performance comparison (RF best)
main_findings: Aggregate explanations miss individual variability; instance-level explainability helps understand decisions but is insufficient for individualized support without theory/context; mispredictions highlight spurious predictors.
conclusions: Explanations alone don’t ensure trustworthy educational decision support; hybrid human-AI collaboration is needed to interpret, contextualize, and act on predictions.
limitations: Online data/ML may not align with pedagogy/theory; explanations can be transparent yet not useful; data-driven recommendations can be implausible; richer data/idiographic approaches may be needed for personalization.

title: Ethical considerations about artificial intelligence for prognostication in intensive care
domain: healthcare; ICU prognostication; ethics; trustworthy AI
user_group: intensivists; patients and families; hospital systems
task: identify ethical challenges of AI-based prognostic models in ICU and propose an ethical implementation pathway
model: AI prognostication models (ANNs, random forests, SVMs, recurrent networks) with emphasis on uncertainty quantification and transparency
explanation_techniques: transparency/interpretability as ethical requirement for autonomy (ability to explain predictions); uncertainty quantification as safety/beneficence requirement; bias auditing as justice requirement
evaluation_design: ethics/review paper proposing checklist and implementation pathway (no primary user study)
metrics_trust: trust framed as public trust maintained via continuous oversight and transparent model governance
metrics_understanding: understanding via transparency/explainability for clinicians/patients in shared decision-making
main_findings: Argues that ICU prognostication AI raises distinct ethical risks because predictions concern future events under interventions and shifting conditions; emphasizes that model evaluation must include uncertainty and robustness, and that data/algorithm choices can create systematic bias and injustice. Proposes an ethics-driven implementation pathway and a checklist integrating medical validity, technical validation, transparency, autonomy, and governance to support trustworthy deployment.
conclusions: ICU AI prognostic tools can be valuable but should be implemented with explicit ethical safeguards: bias scrutiny, uncertainty reporting, transparent explanations, and ongoing oversight to protect autonomy and maintain trust.
limitations: Conceptual review without empirical validation of the proposed checklist; focuses on ICU prognostication and may not generalize to all clinical AI; practical transparency/uncertainty methods and regulatory integration remain challenging.

title: A retrieval-augmented chatbot based on GPT-4 provides appropriate differential diagnosis in gastrointestinal radiology: a proof of concept study
domain: retrieval-augmented LLM decision support; radiology differential diagnosis; transparency
task: compare generic GPT-4 vs GI-imaging-aware retrieval-augmented GPT-4 chatbot for differential diagnosis from imaging descriptions
model: GPT-4 + LlamaIndex retrieval over Radiographics Top 10 GI reading list (96 documents); GIA-CB vs generic GPT-4 chatbot
data_modality: text descriptions of imaging findings (fluoroscopy/MRI/CT cases)
explanation_techniques: retrieval augmentation with source citations; rationale + source excerpts; discusses traceability/accountability limitations of generic LLMs
interaction_design: chatbot interface; decision-support with access to consulted source documents
evaluation_design: proof-of-concept evaluation with 50 synthetic cases; Mann–Whitney U test; comparison to senior radiologist ground truth
participant_type: senior radiologist ground truth (no human-subject user study)
metrics_trust: trustworthiness evaluated via source document retrieval and consistency of explanations
metrics_understanding: explanations with rationale and citations to source documents
main_findings: GIA-CB outperforms generic GPT-4 on differential diagnosis from imaging descriptions, and provides transparency by exposing consulted source documents; shows retrieval mechanism quality is critical (one failure attributed to insufficient semantic context from retrieval, though explanations were consistent with retrieved content). Highlights potential for radiology workload support and quality assurance, but notes latency/cost trade-offs and dependence on retrieval/knowledge base quality.
conclusions: Concludes retrieval-augmented GPT-4 chatbots can provide accurate differential diagnoses in GI radiology from text-described findings and improve trustworthiness by supplying rationale and source excerpts, supporting evidence-based decision support.
limitations: Proof-of-concept with synthetic cases and limited knowledge base (Radiographics Top 10 GI list); retrieval errors can mislead; not validated on real-world reports/images or clinical deployment; higher latency/cost than generic GPT-4.

title: Interpretable machine learning: Fundamental principles and 10 grand challenges
domain: interpretable ML; high-stakes decision support; trust and troubleshooting
task: summarize core principles of interpretable ML and outline open technical challenges
model: inherently interpretable models (decision trees/lists/sets, scoring systems, constrained GAMs, case-based reasoning, interpretable RL) and critique of black-box reliance
explanation_techniques: n/a (survey emphasizes inherent interpretability over post-hoc explanations; discusses pitfalls of black-box/post-hoc explanations)
evaluation_design: survey of principles and technical challenges (no primary empirical study)
metrics_trust: trust framed as a key element for AI models in high-stakes use and as motivation for interpretability
main_findings: Provides a strongly opinionated but technically grounded roadmap: clarifies what interpretability means in practice (troubleshooting, avoiding catastrophic failures, enabling accountability) and argues for inherently interpretable models. The 10 grand challenges define research directions spanning discrete optimization for sparse logical models and scoring systems, constrained additive models, modern case-based reasoning, disentanglement, visualization, physics/causal constraints, Rashomon set characterization, and interpretable RL.
conclusions: Interpretable ML should be treated as a core requirement (not an afterthought) for high-stakes settings; advancing the field requires solving concrete optimization and modeling problems that allow accurate yet inherently interpretable models and principled selection among many good models.
limitations: Survey/position nature: not an empirical evaluation; not exhaustive and reflects authors’ priorities; some challenges (e.g., disentanglement) remain debated and difficult to operationalize; translation to specific domains requires additional human-centered evaluation and governance work.

title: Influences on User Trust in Healthcare Artificial Intelligence: A Systematic Review
domain: trust in healthcare AI (HAI); logic model of influences
task: systematically map influences on user trust in healthcare AI
explanation_techniques: trust-influencing factors include transparency, explainability, and understandability; proposes/updates a logic model (no new XAI method)
evaluation_design: systematic review with narrative synthesis and logic-model development
metrics_trust: trust factors organized as human/AI/context influences
main_findings: Finds limited empirical evidence (3 studies) but identifies broad influences on trust: human-related (knowledge, expectations, mental models, self-efficacy, demographics), AI-related (privacy/safety, transparency, design/customizability, explainability/understandability, power-control balance), and context-related (company, media, social network); formalizes relations via an updated logic model.
conclusions: Concludes trust in healthcare AI depends on multiple interacting factors and that evidence is sparse; positions the review as a starting point for studying trust environments and designing trustworthy AI applications.
limitations: Very small included evidence base; heterogeneous study designs; searches restricted to English/German and pre-2020 search window; excludes robotics and may miss adjacent HAI trust contexts.

title: Explainable AI identifies diagnostic cells of genetic AML subtypes
domain: healthcare; hematopathology; AML subtype classification; explainable MIL
task: classify WHO-defined genetic AML subtypes from digitized blood smears and identify diagnostically relevant single cells
model: SCEMILA: Single-Cell based Explainable Multiple Instance Learning with a novel multi-attention module; pre-trained CNN feature extractor + bag-level classifier
data_modality: white blood cell single-cell images from digitized blood smears (144x144 px)
explanation_techniques: inherent explainability via attention-based MIL (ordering single-cell images by subtype-specific attention); quantitative concordance of high-attention cells with expert diagnostic relevance; interactive visualization for exploring patient embeddings and attention
interaction_design: n/a (but includes interactive online visualization tool for exploration of attention/cells)
evaluation_design: supervised learning with patient-level labels only; 5-fold cross validation; evaluation includes AML vs control discrimination and multi-class subtype classification; analysis of attention agreement with expert assessments
participant_type: AML patients (4 genetic subtypes) and healthy stem-cell donors
metrics_performance: F1 score for subtype classification (APL 0.86±0.05; other subtypes reported); perfect AML vs control discrimination; attention concordance with expert relevance
main_findings: SCEMILA achieves strong subtype classification while providing clinically grounded explanations: the attention module surfaces rare pathognomonic cells, and high-attention cells show high concordance with expert diagnostic relevance. The model can also be used as a single-cell classifier to deconvolve smear composition and highlight subtype-specific cell morphologies, enabling case-level transparency and potential workflow acceleration.
conclusions: Attention-based MIL can deliver “inherently explainable” clinical models that align with expert decision criteria and support routine hematopathology by surfacing diagnostically relevant cells and providing patient-level summaries; prospective and multi-center validation is needed before deployment.
limitations: Model trained on only four AML genetic subtypes and blood smears, so learned correlations may not generalize to broader entities or variable lab staining/scanning; extension to bone marrow, more mutations, and larger diverse cohorts needed; requires standardization and integration into lab workflow; prospective trials required and pixel-level XAI remains challenging.

title: Conservatism predicts aversion to consequential Artificial Intelligence
domain: AI attitudes; algorithm aversion; political ideology; perceived risk; trust
task: report comfort/trust in AI across tasks; assess perceived risk; test moderation by task consequentialness; test moral reframing interventions for healthcare AI support
model: AI (general; scenario-based uses such as medical diagnosis, self-driving cars, and healthcare decisions)
data_modality: survey vignettes; rating scales; online experiments
explanation_techniques: moral reframing arguments (purity/tradition/loyalty vs fairness/care) about healthcare AI; no algorithmic explanation techniques
interaction_design: online survey experiments with between-subjects manipulations (task consequentialness and moral-frame messages); Study 2 includes repeated ratings across 27 tasks
evaluation_design: five survey experiments (MTurk and Prolific) using regressions, mediation and moderated-mediation analyses; exclusions via attention checks and speed thresholds
n_participants: Study1:400; Study2:417; Study3:502; Study4:368; Study5:188 (final analyzed)
participant_type: MTurk users; Prolific participants (including self-identified conservatives)
metrics_trust: comfort/trust ratings (0–100 scales for Study 1; trust ratings across tasks; trust in healthcare AI); support for healthcare AI (3 items, alpha=.85 in Study 4)
main_findings: Conservatism robustly predicts lower comfort/trust in AI; perceived risk mediates the effect. The effect is moderated by task consequentialness (present for consequential tasks, not for inconsequential tasks). Moral reframing shifts liberals more than conservatives; among conservatives (Study 5), framing has no effect on support, perceived risk, or trust.
conclusions: Ideological differences shape trust and adoption of consequential AI; perceived risk is a key psychological driver. Messaging interventions via moral reframing may be insufficient to reduce conservative aversion in high-stakes AI domains.
limitations: Self-report and hypothetical scenarios; US online samples (MTurk/Prolific); some effects small; mediation analyses are correlational; limited set of tasks and moral frames; outcomes focus on attitudes rather than observed reliance behavior.

title: Exploring the artificial intelligence “Trust paradox”: Evidence from a survey experiment in the United States
domain: public trust and adoption of AI-enabled technologies; survey experiment; conjoint analysis
task: test whether willingness to use AI-enabled technologies exceeds trust (trust paradox) across domains and identify mechanisms via mediation
model: conjoint analysis (AMCE) + causal mediation analysis on survey experiment
explanation_techniques: n/a (not an XAI algorithm paper; addresses trust, transparency, and black-box concerns at the attitude level)
interaction_design: conjoint choice tasks + follow-on experimental vignettes; respondents rate support and trust per scenario
evaluation_design: conjoint survey (4 choice sets; >5,000 observations) estimating support vs trust gaps by attribute/domain; follow-on survey experiment with causal mediation analyses of mechanisms for support despite low trust
participant_type: US adults sampled via Lucid quota sampling (Oct 7–21, 2022)
metrics_trust: trust rating per AI-enabled technology scenario (5-point scale)
metrics_reliance: support/willingness to use per scenario (5-point scale)
metrics_understanding: mediator beliefs (fear of missing out; optimism; benefits>risks; efficiency)
main_findings: Evidence for a trust paradox where support exceeds trust across domains, largest for AI-enabled police surveillance (high support and support-trust gap). Mediation analyses suggest support despite low trust is explained by perceived benefits/efficiency, optimism about future improvements, and strategic/social calculations (fear of missing out).
conclusions: Adoption governance should consider that people may accept AI systems they do not trust; measuring both trust and willingness-to-use is necessary, and interventions may need to address benefit-risk expectations and perceived utility.
limitations: Hypothetical scenarios and self-reports; online quota sample may limit external validity; domain framing and attribute choices influence responses; mediation relies on assumptions and may be sensitive to measurement and order effects.

title: Examining explainable clinical decision support systems with think aloud protocols
domain: healthcare; oncology; clinical decision support; human-centered XAI; user study (think-aloud)
task: evaluate an explainable CDSS for lung cancer relapse prediction via Think Aloud Protocol (TAP) to understand credibility/utility and refine explanations
model: graph representation learning model (ComplEx-N3) for tumor recurrence risk + post-hoc local example-based explanation subsystem (ExamplE); SHAP described for tabular models (not evaluated here)
data_modality: clinical EHR features rendered in mock UI + audio transcripts
explanation_techniques: post-hoc local example-based explanations using influential similar patients from the training cohort (ExamplE); (context) SHAP for tabular models described
interaction_design: mock CDSS interface; Artifact 1 shown as prediction-only then prediction+explanation; Artifact 2 shown with prediction+explanation; minimal prompting to avoid priming
evaluation_design: remote TAP sessions (Microsoft Teams) with thematic analysis; binary response questions (confusing/overwhelming/missing/useful/misleading) + ecological validity questions (would use/recommend)
participant_type: oncologists (hospital-based; >=5 years specialization training post-graduation)
metrics_trust: credibility theme; binary questions about confusion/overwhelm/misleading/useful; strong desire for model uncertainty/performance reporting
metrics_reliance: n/a (no behavioral reliance task); ecological validity: intention to use/recommend captured post-TAP
metrics_understanding: confusion/understanding of example-based explanation mechanism (only 3/10 understood); “concept/execution gap” theme
metrics_performance: n/a for the user study; underlying model accuracy reported as 68% on a 200-patient held-out test set
main_findings: Five themes emerged: concept/execution gap, manifestation of process, perception of credibility, perception of utility, and frozen vs. dynamic. Most participants found the example-based explanation confusing or misleading (7/10), wanted prioritized clinical features and uncertainty/accuracy cues (e.g., confidence intervals), and questioned the representativeness of showing only 2–3 “influential examples” from a 1,000+ cohort; explanations sometimes helped make the score feel “supported by data” but did not clearly justify the prediction or next clinical action.
conclusions: TAP provides a low-cost, low-priming method to surface clinician reasoning about XAI CDSS outputs and can guide iterative redesign; explanation formats should better align with clinicians’ expectations (salient variables, uncertainty, clinical workflow fit) to support adoption and calibrated trust.
limitations: Potential contamination (all participants from one hospital); TAP may not capture full cognition under high cognitive load and relies on comfort with verbalization; sessions conducted in English with mostly Spanish-speaking clinicians may reduce expression fidelity.

title: Explainable artificial intelligence in emergency medicine: an overview
venue: Clinical and Experimental Emergency Medicine (Review Article)
domain: emergency medicine; clinical AI adoption; explainability/trust
user_group: emergency clinicians; AI developers in emergency care
task: overview of XAI concepts, categories, and challenges in emergency medicine
model: AI/ML models in emergency medicine (triage, diagnosis, prognostication); includes interpretable and post-hoc explainers
data_modality: clinical tabular data; EHR; imaging; time series (context-dependent)
explanation_techniques: pre-modeling explainability (data visualization/summarization); interpretable models (decomposability/simulatable models; hybrid interpretable approaches like AutoScore); post-modeling explainability: visualization, textual justification, simplification (e.g., surrogate decision tree for ensembles), feature relevance (LIME, SHAP)
interaction_design: clinician-facing explanation requirements; emphasizes audience/context-dependent explainability needs; recommends multidisciplinary collaboration
evaluation_design: clinical overview/survey with examples and taxonomy
metrics_trust: trust issues from black-box perception; need justification/control for clinical acceptance
metrics_understanding: improved understanding via interpretable/post-hoc explanations; context-specific explanation needs
main_findings: Provides a clinician-oriented taxonomy of XAI for emergency medicine and argues that trust and reliance require clarifying distinct notions (transparency vs interpretability vs explainability). Highlights that explanation needs differ by context and user role; presents examples of post-hoc methods (SHAP/LIME, visualization, text justifications) and hybrid interpretable approaches that balance performance and interpretability. Emphasizes implementation barriers in time-critical, high-stakes workflows and calls for collaboration among clinicians, developers, and researchers to create usable, trustworthy systems.
conclusions: Concludes XAI can mitigate trust barriers to deploying AI/ML in emergency medicine by making model behavior comprehensible for justification and control, but successful translation requires aligning explanation approaches with clinical workflows and collaborative design/deployment practices.
limitations: n/a (review/overview; no explicit limitations section identified in PDF text extraction)

title: Explainable Recommendation: A Survey and New Perspectives
venue: Foundations and Trends in Information Retrieval
domain: explainable recommender systems; explanation types; evaluation; trust/persuasion
task: survey explainable recommendation methods, explanation information sources, model families, and evaluation approaches
model: survey taxonomy of explainable recommendation (factorization, topic models, graphs, deep learning, knowledge graphs, rule mining, post-hoc/model-agnostic)
data_modality: recommender data (ratings, text reviews, item/user features, graphs) discussed as sources; no new dataset
explanation_techniques: explanation information sources: relevant user/item, feature-based, opinion-based, sentence, visual, social; model classes: factorization, topic modeling, graph, deep, knowledge-graph, rule mining, post-hoc/model-agnostic; evaluation: user study, online, offline, case study
evaluation_design: survey (101 pages) synthesizing methods and evaluation protocols; summarizes open directions including evaluation, user behavior analysis, and cognitive science foundations
metrics_trust: trustworthiness/satisfaction as targets of explanation evaluation (surveyed)
metrics_understanding: user understanding/persuasion as explanation goals and evaluation dimensions (surveyed)
metrics_performance: recommender effectiveness metrics discussed; evaluation trade-offs
main_findings: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy of explainable recommendation methods and interfaces, clarifying how explanation sources map to model designs and evaluation. Highlights that evaluation remains underdeveloped: requires reliable measures across user-centric outcomes (trust, satisfaction, persuasiveness) and algorithm-centric qualities, and cautions about bias/limitations of user studies and online tests.
conclusions: Positions explainability as a “why” component of recommender systems, outlining best-known method families and a forward agenda: better evaluation, broader application domains, and stronger cognitive science foundations for explanations.
limitations: Survey scope limited to literature up to 2020; heterogeneity of definitions and evaluation measures makes comparisons difficult; many methods lack standardized evaluation.

title: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations
venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (AAAI-18)
domain: XAI methods; local explanations; human evaluation of interpretability
task: test whether anchor rule explanations improve humans’ ability to predict model behavior on unseen instances
model: Anchors (high-precision IF-THEN rules with probabilistic guarantees) vs LIME linear explanations vs no explanations; applied to black-box models
data_modality: tabular datasets (Adult, recidivism) + VQA multiple-choice (images/questions) user tasks
explanation_techniques: anchor rules with explicit coverage; comparisons to LIME and submodular pick (SP-Anchor/SP-LIME) to choose representative explanation sets
evaluation_design: user study: predict model answers on 20 instances before/after explanations; measure human precision/coverage and time; post-study preference poll
participant_type: students who had or were taking a machine learning course
metrics_trust: human interpretability proxy (confidence/poll preference); no explicit trust instrument
metrics_understanding: human precision of predicting model outputs; “I don’t know” usage as calibration/coverage measure
metrics_performance: time to understand/use explanations; human precision (~95% with anchors)
main_findings: Across datasets/tasks, anchors produced high human precision (~95%) with lower time/effort than LIME; LIME precision varied and could be worse than no explanations in some settings (e.g., VQA). Most participants preferred anchors (21/26) and believed they would be more precise with anchors (24/26).
conclusions: High-precision, coverage-aware explanations can improve human understanding of model behavior and reduce incorrect generalization; rule-based anchors are a strong alternative to linear feature-attribution explanations for model-agnostic local interpretability.
limitations: Anchors can become overly specific near decision boundaries or for rare classes (low coverage); possible (rare) conflicting anchors may apply to the same instance; explanation quality depends on the perturbation distribution (design remains challenging); complex structured outputs may require broader explanation families.

title: Does Explainable Artificial Intelligence Improve Human Decision-Making?
venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (AAAI-21)
domain: human-AI decision-making; empirical evaluation; anchor-based explanations
task: test whether adding XAI (anchor LIME) improves human decision accuracy beyond AI predictions alone
model: black-box predictors: MLP (Census Income) and SVM-RBF (COMPAS); explanations via anchor LIME (rule-based local sufficient conditions)
data_modality: tabular vignettes (Census income, COMPAS) presented as profiles
explanation_techniques: anchor LIME explanations (high-precision rule “anchors” clarifying local coverage) presented alongside AI predictions
interaction_design: per-trial decision aid: show profile + (optional) AI prediction and (optional) anchor LIME explanation; participants choose label and rate confidence; immediate correctness feedback
evaluation_design: 2 (dataset: Census/COMPAS) x 3 (condition: control/AI/AI+explanation) between-subjects; 50 test trials per participant; AI accuracy varied per participant; outcomes include accuracy, confidence calibration, adherence, and reaction time
participant_type: Amazon Mechanical Turk participants (50 per condition; recruited via Volunteer Science)
metrics_trust: n/a (no explicit trust scale; confidence used as proxy)
metrics_reliance: adherence to AI recommendation; sensitivity to AI correctness
metrics_understanding: confidence calibration; ability to detect when AI correct
metrics_performance: n/a (classification accuracy and reaction time)
main_findings: AI predictions improved human accuracy vs control (especially on Census), but adding anchor LIME explanations did not provide a meaningful improvement over AI-only. AI accuracy was the strongest predictor of human performance. Participants tended to follow AI more when it was correct; AI (with or without explanations) increased confidence that was on average reasonably calibrated.
conclusions: Objective human performance should be evaluated directly; explanations that are interpretable may not translate into improved decisions. In some tasks, decision support via predictions helps while “why” information may not add benefit; integrating XAI into real systems requires further research.
limitations: Limited generalizability across datasets/tasks/explanation methods; uses one XAI technique (anchor LIME) and simplified 2-choice decisions; explanation effectiveness may depend on dataset/task format and may differ with experts or probabilistic settings.

title: Delivering Trustworthy AI through Formal XAI
venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (AAAI-22)
domain: formal XAI; trustworthy AI; explanation guarantees
user_group: high-stakes ML stakeholders (regulators, auditors, impacted individuals, developers)
task: argue for formal explanations with guarantees (soundness + minimality) and survey progress/challenges in formal XAI
model: Formal XAI for classifiers (NNs, decision trees/ensembles, etc.) via formally defined explanations: abductive explanations (AXp, “why”) and contrastive explanations (CXp, “why not”) computed with decision procedures (SAT/SMT, compiled forms)
data_modality: model internals (logical constraints over features); examples include neural network encodings
explanation_techniques: formal explanations with guarantees: minimal sufficient feature sets (AXp) and minimal contrastive sets (CXp); soundness and irredundancy; links to fairness and robustness reasoning
evaluation_design: survey/position paper summarizing formal definitions, computation (hitting sets, oracle-guided search), and research challenges; includes motivating examples of explanation failure in non-formal methods
metrics_trust: trust framed as requiring sound explanations and avoiding misleading/contradictory justifications
main_findings: Shows that non-formal/model-agnostic explanations can be inconsistent or misleading in high-risk settings, motivating formal definitions. Formal XAI provides explanations that are provably sufficient for the prediction and minimal (irredundant), enabling rigorous auditing and reducing spurious “reasons.” Surveys progress on computing explanations for NNs and other models with SAT/SMT and related techniques and highlights open challenges (scalability, explanation size/complexity, extending beyond classification).
conclusions: Formal XAI is positioned as a necessary foundation for trustworthy AI in high-risk deployments, enabling rigorous, minimal, and sound explanations that support accountability and regulation.
limitations: Formal methods can be computationally heavy and may yield large explanations; scalability for complex models (notably deep NNs) remains challenging; most results focus on classification and require faithful model encodings and assumptions about distributions/drift.

title: The Effects of Explanations in Automated Essay Scoring Systems on Student Trust and Motivation
domain: education; learning analytics; automated essay scoring; transparency; trust
task: write an argumentative essay; receive an AES grade with/without explanation; report trust and motivation; optionally adjust own grade
model: Wizard-of-Oz AES (rule-based grading in LimeSurvey, presented as AI)
explanation_techniques: full-text global explanations (student needs-elicitation + XAI guidelines); accuracy statement (confidence score); no explanation
interaction_design: between-subjects (3 conditions); explanations shown with system grade; post-task questionnaires; participants could re-estimate their grade and indicate willingness to continue writing
evaluation_design: lab/online experiment with university students; 3 conditions; measures: Trust in Automation scale (TiA), objective Weight-of-Advice (grade adjustment), Intrinsic Motivation Inventory (interest/enjoyment), willingness to continue; regression models with covariates
participant_type: university students (TU Eindhoven participant pool)
metrics_trust: TiA scale (12 items, 1–7, alpha=.88); trust also via WoA grade adjustment
metrics_reliance: WoA / % changing grade toward system; willingness to continue writing (binary)
main_findings: Explanation type did not significantly predict subjective trust or WoA; trust is driven by system outcome, especially mismatch between self-estimated and system grades (nonlinear). Most participants moved their grade toward the system (54–69%). Accuracy-statement condition showed a small increase in subjective motivation (interest/enjoyment) vs no explanation, but no reliable effect on willingness to continue.
conclusions: In AES, generic global explanations or simple accuracy statements may not be sufficient to increase trust/motivation; the system’s outcome dominates user perceptions. Explanation design should be context- and stakeholder-specific rather than one-size-fits-all.
limitations: Wizard-of-Oz (not a real AES model); single short interaction; student-only sample; limited explanation designs; relatively low motivation suggests limited engagement; ecological validity constraints.

title: A Survey of the State of Explainable AI for Natural Language Processing
domain: survey (XAI for NLP)
user_group: model developers; end users of NLP systems
task: survey of explainability techniques and evaluation gaps for NLP
model: NLP models (white-box to deep learning; attention/transformers; post-hoc explainers)
explanation_techniques: feature importance; attention; saliency/gradients; surrogate explainers (e.g., LIME); example-based/neighbors; rule/extraction; (survey taxonomy)
interaction_design: primarily developer-facing explanations; highlights outcome explanation and user trust/feedback loops
evaluation_design: survey of 50 papers from major NLP conferences 2013–2019 (title-based filtering; double-review classification); companion website
metrics_trust: frames explanations as supporting user trust and feedback
metrics_understanding: evaluation gap noted; calls for formal definitions and evaluation methods
main_findings: Synthesizes NLP-XAI literature and provides taxonomy: local vs global and self-explaining vs post-hoc, plus five major technique families for producing justifications. Identifies key gaps: inconsistent definitions of explainability and limited evaluation methodology; provides a curated resource (interactive website) to track techniques and papers.
conclusions: Encourages stronger evaluation and clearer definitions for explainability in NLP; positions explanations as a tool for trust and actionable feedback to improve model quality.
limitations: Search strategy is title-keyword based and not exhaustive; survey focuses on outcome explanations and excludes broader behavioral explanations.

title: Applications and Challenges of Implementing Artificial Intelligence in Medical Education: Integrative Review
domain: AI in medical education; adoption challenges; curriculum integration
task: review AI applications and implementation challenges in medical education
model: AI in education systems (learning support/assessment)
explanation_techniques: AI applications for guided learning pathways and feedback; adoption analysis via TAM/DOI constructs; not focused on XAI methods
evaluation_design: integrative review with quantitative pooling and adoption-theory framing
metrics_trust: trust not central; touches on data integrity and acceptability factors
metrics_reliance: acceptance/adoption framing via TAM/DOI
metrics_understanding: understanding AI algorithms via curriculum integration recommendation
main_findings: Finds AI in medical education is used primarily for learning support due to individualized feedback; limited use for assessment/curriculum review due to digitalization and exam sensitivity; main barriers are assessing AI effectiveness and technical challenges; calls for methodological improvements and teaching AI literacy.
conclusions: Concludes AI adoption in medical education can be improved by addressing technical barriers and developing better evaluation methods; recommends introducing AI into medical curricula so professionals understand algorithms and maximize safe use.
limitations: Review up to March 2019; heterogeneous study designs and variable evidence quality; focuses on education rather than clinical outcomes.

title: Psychosocial Factors Affecting Artificial Intelligence Adoption in Health Care in China: Cross-Sectional Study
domain: public acceptance of medical AI; technology adoption; trust moderation
user_group: general public (China); future: clinicians as users
task: model psychosocial factors influencing intention to use ophthalmic AI screening devices
model: SEM (technology acceptance model variant) over constructs incl. subjective norms, perceived usefulness/ease, perceived behavioral control, resistance bias, trust
explanation_techniques: n/a (acceptance study; not explanation algorithms)
evaluation_design: cross-sectional survey + CFA/SEM; mediation and moderation analysis
participant_type: general public respondents (China; internet-recruited)
metrics_trust: trust as moderator of adoption; acceptance/intention-to-use as trust-adjacent construct
metrics_understanding: perceived ease/usefulness understanding of device function
metrics_performance: n/a (predictive explanatory power via R2; model fit indices)
main_findings: Shows social/psychological drivers of intended adoption of ophthalmic AI devices: subjective norms and perceived usefulness increase intention, resistance bias reduces it; perceived behavioral control and norms have indirect effects via perceived usefulness/ease. Trust plays a moderating role (higher trust changes how usefulness translates into intention), and authors argue cultural context (collectivism/Confucian norms) shapes adoption dynamics.
conclusions: Concludes the proposed SEM explains public acceptance and intention to use ophthalmic AI devices in China with good fit and R2, and suggests stakeholders should avoid misleading or exaggerated claims when promoting healthcare AI.
limitations: Online/mobile/web recruitment yielded relatively few participants above age 50; older adults may have lower digital skills and may not be appropriate target users without usability adaptation; future work should test age moderation and study clinician adoption in real clinical scenarios.

title: Conversational Agents in Health Care: Scoping Review and Conceptual Analysis
venue: J Med Internet Res (Review; Scoping review)
domain: conversational agents (chatbots) in healthcare; adoption/safety/acceptability
task: scoping review of healthcare chatbot applications, gaps, challenges
model: conversational agents (mostly AI-driven; text-based; smartphone apps)
data_modality: text (free text in/out); some multimodal interfaces
explanation_techniques: n/a (not XAI-focused); highlights "black box" unpredictability risks for ML-driven agents; calls for transparency and regulation
interaction_design: conversational UI; mobile delivery; free-text input/output; discusses chatbot personality traits and integration into hybrid care models
evaluation_design: scoping review with thematic analysis; 47 reports (45 papers + 2 ongoing trials); identifies app modalities and use-cases
metrics_trust: acceptability and trust concerns; safety risk from inappropriate advice; privacy concerns; need regulatory certification
metrics_understanding: transparency/understanding gaps (black-box effect for ML-based agents)
metrics_performance: effectiveness evidence limited; calls for robust evaluation across formats
main_findings: Finds most published healthcare conversational agents are text-based, ML-driven, and smartphone-delivered; evidence base is largely descriptive (many development case studies) with relatively few RCTs. Highlights risks relevant to trust: inappropriate advice in high-stakes contexts, black-box unpredictability, and privacy issues for free-text input. Recommends future work to evaluate feasibility/acceptability/safety/effectiveness across diverse formats, develop clearer guidance for development/evaluation, increase geographic diversity (LMIC evidence scarce), and assess sustainable integration into existing health systems (hybrid human-supported models).
