conclusions: Concludes conversational agents are promising but not robustly assessed; future research should prioritize acceptability, safety, effectiveness, and guidance for development/evaluation aligned with target populations and health-system integration.
limitations: May omit unpublished/niche work due to terminology/novelty; theme classification may be incomplete and will evolve; excluded poorly reported assessment studies; developed an exploratory framework adapted for this review.

title: Digital Natives’ Preferences on Mobile Artificial Intelligence Apps for Skin Cancer Diagnostics: Survey Study
domain: healthcare; dermatology; mobile health; adoption/trust; patient/user preferences
task: assess acceptance, preferences, and concerns for AI-based mobile skin cancer diagnostic apps
model: n/a (preference study; app attributes include accuracy, encryption, explainability/transparency, role in care)
data_modality: survey (German-language) with conjoint and MaxDiff tasks
explanation_techniques: transparency/explainability requirement: background information about decision-making process; preference for “reasoning for the decision” and information delivery (not a specific XAI algorithm)
interaction_design: n/a (survey)
evaluation_design: anonymous online survey; adaptive choice-based conjoint (hierarchical Bayes) + MaxDiff scaling; descriptive stats and subgroup tests
participant_type: young adults (<35), recruited via Facebook/LinkedIn/Xing (Germany; survey in German)
metrics_trust: attitude toward AI app use; concerns include “insufficient trust in the app” (MaxDiff)
metrics_reliance: intended use/preferences for app role (assistance vs stand-alone); acceptability thresholds (e.g., accuracy)
metrics_understanding: basic understanding of decision-making via explanations/transparency demanded
main_findings: Most respondents were open to AI skin-cancer apps, but acceptance is conditional: participants rejected low-accuracy apps (≤65%), unencrypted data storage, and non-transparent systems without background info on decision-making. Negative respondents prioritized data security, low trust, and lack of human interaction; positive respondents preferred AI as an assistance system and accepted anonymous data use for research.
conclusions: For successful clinical translation of AI mHealth diagnostics, designers should emphasize accuracy, data security, and explainability/transparency, and position apps as augmenting clinician care rather than replacing it.
limitations: Sample predominantly female; recruitment via social media risks sampling bias and limits generalizability; attribute importance depends on chosen levels/features (conjoint design dependence); MaxDiff yields relative (not absolute) concern magnitudes.

title: Population Preferences for Performance and Explainability of Artificial Intelligence in Health Care: Choice-Based Conjoint Survey
domain: public preferences; explainability vs performance trade-offs; discrimination risk
task: estimate public preferences for attributes of AI decision support in hospitals
model: n/a (conjoint scenarios)
explanation_techniques: explanation level as conjoint attribute; transparency/explainability framed as potentially trading off with performance; includes attribute for discrimination testing
evaluation_design: choice-based conjoint survey (12 tasks; 100 fractional factorial choice sets; focus group informed)
participant_type: adult Danish population (representative sample)
metrics_trust: trust in health and technology measured but largely not influential in utilities
metrics_performance: preference utilities/attribute importance (conjoint)
main_findings: Finds physician final responsibility dominates preferences (~46.8% weight), followed by explainability (~27.3%) and discrimination testing (~14.8%); demographic and attitudinal factors (trust/fear/hope) generally do not change preferences; implies policy should prioritize accountability and explainability.
conclusions: Concludes public policy should ensure AI decision support in healthcare keeps physicians ultimately responsible, provides explainability, and is tested for discrimination, with patient information provision.
limitations: Country-specific (Denmark) and stated-preference design; scenarios and attribute wording may affect results; does not measure real behavior or clinical outcomes.

title: The Impact of Explanations on Layperson Trust in Artificial Intelligence–Driven Symptom Checker Apps: Experimental Study
domain: lay trust; symptom checkers; explanation types; knowledge effects
task: test whether explanation types affect explanatory trust in AI symptom checkers
model: AI-driven chatbot symptom checker (simulated)
data_modality: video-based chatbot diagnosis scenarios (migraine vs temporal arteritis)
explanation_techniques: explanation types: why- vs how-style and contrastive/counterfactual-inspired forms; includes social proof and input-influence style explanations (as described in results section)
interaction_design: explanations shown alongside chatbot diagnosis in video
evaluation_design: vignette-based experimental survey with between-group explanation and disease manipulations
participant_type: healthy adult participants
metrics_trust: explanatory trust (factor-derived measures) and related trust constructs
metrics_reliance: likelihood to follow instructions implied as adoption condition (discussion)
metrics_understanding: mental model/prior knowledge variables derived via factor analysis
main_findings: Finds explanations matter more when participants lack prior knowledge (temporal arteritis): different explanation types yield different trust; when knowledge is high (migraine), explanation type has little effect. Suggests tailoring explanations to user questions/knowledge and closing information gaps is key for effective symptom checker adoption.
conclusions: Concludes explanation designers should personalize explanations to users’ disease knowledge and needs; one-size-fits-all explanations may not improve trust for familiar conditions but can affect trust when information need is high.
limitations: Video/vignette study with healthy participants; measures trust rather than clinical accuracy; explanation quality limited to selected formats and may not generalize to interactive real-world symptom checker use.

title: Artificial Intelligence Applications in Health Care Practice: Scoping Review
domain: healthcare AI implementation; trust/transparency barriers
task: map empirical evidence on AI implementation in routine healthcare practice
data_modality: multiple clinical settings and AI systems (implementation studies)
explanation_techniques: discusses need for explainable/interpretable solutions and transparency to build trust; not a method paper
evaluation_design: scoping review (MEDLINE/Scopus/Web of Science/CINAHL/PsycINFO + snowballing)
metrics_trust: building trust; transparency issues; ethical concerns (privacy/data protection)
metrics_performance: effectiveness focus of included studies (intervention outcomes)
main_findings: Finds most implemented AI systems have low action autonomy and support human decision-making; implementation research prioritizes effectiveness and technical aspects over process details; frameworks guiding implementation are rare; calls out trust, transparency, explainability, and ethics as AI-unique implementation concerns needing more empirical study.
conclusions: Concludes empirical knowledge is dominated by low-autonomy AI implementations; further work should study more disruptive systems and develop an empirically grounded, AI-specific implementation framework including trust/transparency and ethical considerations.
limitations: Limited to published empirical implementation studies; heterogeneous domains and outcomes; implementation processes often under-reported, limiting synthesis and generalizability.

title: Trust in and Acceptance of Artificial Intelligence Applications in Medicine: Mixed Methods Study
domain: trust and acceptance factors; stakeholder perspectives
user_group: medical AI stakeholders (researchers, tech providers, hospital staff, policy makers)
task: identify and rate relevance of factors driving trust and acceptance in medical AI
model: n/a (factor framework)
explanation_techniques: explainability and transparency of AI processes/outcomes as key technology-related factors; ethics/legal factors include data use transparency, accountability, equity/fairness; framework of 19 umbrella factors
evaluation_design: rapid review + stakeholder Likert-scale e-survey
participant_type: stakeholders (mostly researchers; also providers, hospital staff, policy makers)
metrics_trust: trust and acceptance relevance ratings (Likert)
main_findings: Finds broad set of factors influences trust/acceptance; most umbrella factors rated highly relevant; emphasizes transparency/explainability, accountability, privacy, fairness, and workflow integration; patient demographics seen as less relevant by surveyed stakeholders.
conclusions: Concludes factor framework can guide implementers to design strategies to facilitate trust and acceptance among key stakeholders and potential users of medical AI.
limitations: Small and unbalanced survey sample (researcher-heavy); factor synthesis depends on rapid review scope; measures perceived relevance rather than observed adoption outcomes.

title: Large Language Models for Therapy Recommendations Across 3 Clinical Specialties: Comparative Study
domain: LLM medical advice quality and safety; trust calibration
task: evaluate quality/correctness/safety of therapy recommendations from multiple LLMs
model: Claude-instant-v1.0; GPT-3.5-Turbo; Command-xlarge-nightly; Bloomz; GPT-4 for automated evaluation
data_modality: text prompts and LLM-generated text recommendations
explanation_techniques: n/a (not XAI-focused); uses GPT-4 as scalable auto-evaluator; assesses falseness/harmfulness and information quality
evaluation_design: comparative evaluation study (3 physicians rating 4 LLMs across 60 diseases) + GPT-4 automated scoring; ANOVA/t-tests; correlation analysis
participant_type: domain-specific physicians
metrics_trust: harmfulness/safety ratings; correctness/falseness as trust-relevant reliability cues
metrics_performance: mDISCERN (information quality); correctness; falseness; harmfulness
main_findings: Finds large variation in medical content quality and safety across LLMs and specialties: Claude-instant-v1.0 highest mDISCERN, Bloomz lowest; GPT-3.5-Turbo lowest harmfulness. Error patterns include wrong/confused diagnoses, vague advice, and omission of critical treatments. LLMs under-report risks and QoL impacts and rarely provide sources. GPT-4 auto-evaluation aligns substantially with physician ratings, suggesting scalable assessment workflows.
conclusions: Concludes LLMs show promise for generating medical therapy information but require regular, methodical evaluation, oversight, and fine-tuning to ensure trustworthy and clinically safe advice; proposes GPT-4-based auto-evaluation as a scalable method.
limitations: Limited specialties and physician evaluators; simple prompt strategy; benchmarks designed for human-authored content may miss AI nuances; rapidly changing LLMs may limit generalizability over time.

title: Existing Barriers Faced by and Future Design Recommendations for Direct-to-Consumer Health Care Artificial Intelligence Apps: Scoping Review
venue: J Med Internet Res (Review; Scoping review)
domain: direct-to-consumer (DTC) health AI apps; human-centered explainability; calibrated trust
user_group: consumers/patients; some apps connect to clinicians/family/nursing/health departments
task: scoping review of barriers and design recommendations for DTC healthcare AI apps
model: DTC healthcare AI apps (often ML-based decision support/diagnosis/self-management)
data_modality: mobile apps; consumer-facing UI; mixed modalities (often text/app input/output)
explanation_techniques: human-centered explainability; transparency; calibrated trust and overtrust mitigation
interaction_design: empathy and communication design; consumer-facing decision support; connections/alerts to clinicians/family/staff
evaluation_design: scoping review + thematic analysis; 2898 retrieved -> 32 included
metrics_trust: calibrated trust; overtrust/undertrust; consumer safety concerns
metrics_understanding: explainability needs for consumer understanding and responsible use
metrics_performance: n/a (review synthesizes risks rather than benchmarking accuracy)
main_findings: Synthesizes evidence on DTC healthcare AI apps and identifies barriers and design recommendations, emphasizing particularly human-centered explainability, calibrated trust (and addressing overtrust), demonstrating empathy, improving product specialization, and expanding diversity of test populations. Reports most studies are recent (2018-2023) and concentrated in developed countries; common app functions include diagnosis and self-management, with some designs connecting consumers to clinicians or caregivers for alerts.
conclusions: Concludes DTC healthcare AI apps create both opportunities and risks; provides a structured map of study characteristics plus barrier/recommendation taxonomy to guide future research and consumer-facing design.
limitations: English-language retrieval only may overlook influential work in other languages.

title: How Explainable Artificial Intelligence Can Increase or Decrease Clinicians’ Trust in AI Applications in Health Care: Systematic Review
domain: clinician trust; explanation quality; calibration and over/under-trust
task: synthesize empirical evidence on XAI impact on clinician trust
explanation_techniques: explanations as intervention; highlights that clear, concise, clinically relevant explanations can increase trust, while complex/contradictory ones can undermine trust; discusses cognitive-based vs affect-based trust operationalizations
evaluation_design: systematic review with risk-of-bias assessment (RoB2/ROBINS-I)
participant_type: practicing clinicians (in included studies)
metrics_trust: cognition- and affect-based trust measures (varies by study)
metrics_reliance: trust calibration (avoid overtrust/distrust)
metrics_understanding: understanding implied via explanation clarity/coherence/relevance
metrics_performance: clinical accuracy harms from miscalibrated trust (discussion)
main_findings: Finds 5 studies report increased trust with XAI (when explanations are clear/concise/relevant), 3 show no significant effect, and 2 show trust can increase or decrease depending on explanation complexity/coherence; notes all included studies have moderate to moderate-high bias risk and that explanation presence alone is insufficient.
conclusions: Concludes trust must be optimized (avoid overtrust and distrust) and future work should refine trust measures (cognitive + affect) and develop strategies to calibrate trust for effective clinical integration of AI.
limitations: Only 10 included studies; moderate/high risk of bias; heterogeneous definitions/measures of trust and XAI; evidence base still small and mostly experimental rather than real-world deployment.

title: Trust and Acceptance Challenges in the Adoption of AI Applications in Health Care: Quantitative Survey Analysis
domain: healthcare AI adoption; trust and acceptance; individual differences
task: quantify trust and acceptance drivers across healthcare AI use cases
model: gradient boosted tree models (analysis) + SHAP explanations
data_modality: web-based survey responses across 8 AI use cases
explanation_techniques: SHAP feature attribution applied to gradient boosted trees (explaining predictors of trust/acceptance); discusses context/risk and interaction effects
evaluation_design: quantitative web-based survey + predictive modeling interpretability analysis
participant_type: Finnish adult participants
metrics_trust: trust ratings per use case; intention to use; willingness to trade personal data
main_findings: Finds that technology attitudes/use and personality traits are primary drivers of trust and intention to use, more than the specific use case; noninvasive monitors are most accepted; observes nonlinear patterns (e.g., inverted-U relationship between self-reported AI knowledge and positivity) and demographic differences (women more cautious).
conclusions: Concludes trust and acceptance are shaped by a complex interplay of individual factors; recommends considering demographics, personality, and technology attitudes when designing and deploying healthcare AI, and positions predictive models as decision-support tools for implementation planning.
limitations: Survey-based and country-specific (Finland); attitudes may not translate directly to real-world usage; causal claims limited; measures depend on scenario descriptions and self-report.

title: Investigating Whether AI Will Replace Human Physicians and Understanding the Interplay of the Source of Consultation, Health-Related Stigma, and Explanations of Diagnoses on Patients’ Evaluations of Medical Consultations: Randomized Factorial Experiment
domain: patient-centered evaluation of AI vs humans; diagnosis explanations; trust and algorithm aversion
task: measure how consultation source, stigma, and explanations affect functional/relational/emotional evaluations
model: medical agents: AI vs human-involved AI vs human physician
explanation_techniques: explanation provision (with vs without explanation) as an intervention; AI is treated as black-box by default (no specific XAI method described in abstract section)
interaction_design: diagnosis explanation shown/withheld in consultation scenarios
evaluation_design: randomized factorial experiment with between-subject factors
participant_type: adult participants (web-based sample)
metrics_trust: functional trust in diagnosis (ratings) + related evaluation scales
metrics_reliance: acceptance of AI vs human diagnosis (preference via evaluations)
metrics_understanding: relational/emotional evaluation of interaction and perceived communication quality
main_findings: Shows algorithm aversion in diagnosis: higher functional trust for human physicians than AI/human-involved AI; stigma manipulation does not increase preference for AI even for stigmatized diseases (instrumental diagnostic needs override privacy/anonymity assumptions); providing explanations significantly improves functional, relational, and emotional evaluations for all consultation sources.
conclusions: Concludes that, currently, human expertise is trusted more than accurate AI for diagnosis, but explanation provision can improve evaluation and adherence-related outcomes; suggests user-centered design should integrate explanations to enhance communication in AI-mediated care.
limitations: Vignette-based and self-report; specific explanation content/quality not deeply characterized in the abstract; sample/context may limit generalizability to real clinical encounters and higher-stakes decisions.

title: Co-Design of a Trustworthy AI System in Healthcare: Deep Learning Based Skin Lesion Classifier
domain: healthcare; dermatology; trustworthy AI; co-design
user_group: dermatologists, multidisciplinary experts, AI engineers/managers (patients planned in later phases)
task: co-design and early-stage assessment of a skin lesion classifier explanation add-on (exAID) to support trustworthy AI
model: deep learning skin lesion classifier + explanation module (exAID add-on); interpretability via heatmaps/saliency/attention discussed
explanation_techniques: saliency/heatmap localization to link model evidence to clinically meaningful criteria; attention-based highlighting; SHAP-family methods referenced; emphasis on interpretability as one component within a broader trustworthy-AI co-design/inspection process
interaction_design: three Zoom co-design workshops; socio-technical scenario discussions; parallel small working groups (3–5 experts) and plenary synthesis; iterative refinement of the system aim
evaluation_design: qualitative co-design case study using Z-Inspection; mapping tensions/trade-offs to EU AI HLEG trustworthy AI requirements and redesigning the stated purpose/guardrails
participant_type: interdisciplinary experts (incl dermatologists, ethicists, policy makers, legal/data protection specialists, social scientists, computer scientists, ML engineers)
metrics_trust: qualitative trustworthiness assessment via requirement elicitation, tensions/trade-offs, and governance/guardrail discussions
main_findings: Co-design surfaced key pitfalls early: dataset and scope constraints (e.g., bias and limited representativeness), risk of overdiagnosis/defensive overtreatment, and the need for clear guardrails and communication for clinicians/patients; the interdisciplinary process led stakeholders to redefine the system aim and clarify accountability and oversight responsibilities.
conclusions: Holistic, multidisciplinary co-design/inspection can improve the trustworthiness of healthcare AI before deployment by aligning goals, surfacing socio-technical risks, and informing interpretability and governance choices; future phases should include patient peer groups and field evaluation.
limitations: Early design-phase study (not a controlled end-user trial); difficult to enumerate exhaustive evaluation criteria during workshops; Z-Inspection is a discussion process rather than an ethical approval/audit; patient peer group not included in this phase.

title: User perspectives on AI explainability in aerospace manufacturing: a Card-Sorting study
domain: human-centered XAI; explainable decision support; aerospace manufacturing
user_group: aerospace manufacturing professionals (high-level managers; prospective DSS end-users)
task: categorize and rank AI-based DSS features that enhance/are neutral/hinder explainability
model: AI-based decision support systems (generic; feature-level study)
explanation_techniques: feature-level XAI requirements: transparency of algorithms; decision rationale documentation; real-time explanations; contextual data info; user guides/tooltips; error messages; avoiding technical jargon
interaction_design: Closed card sorting in Qualtrics (drag-and-drop) plus within-group ranking (1=most important); sessions in-person/online; audio-recorded verbal rationales
evaluation_design: descriptive stats for categorizations and rankings; thematic analysis of transcripts (Braun & Clarke); triangulation of quantitative and qualitative results
participant_type: aerospace manufacturing experts (11 male, 4 female; mean age 41.6; 5–16 years sector experience; multiple EU sites)
metrics_understanding: perceived explainability priorities; qualitative themes about trust, usability, cognitive load
main_findings: User support features were most consistently seen as enhancing explainability (e.g., tooltips/help, error messages with guidance, system feedback). Technical jargon was a dominant hindrance (10/15). In ranked priorities, transparency of algorithms was highest (median rank 2, IQR 1–3), followed by clarity of interface design (median 3, IQR 1–3) and system feedback on user inputs (median 3.5).
conclusions: For high-stakes industrial DSSs, explainability should be designed around user-centered support and plain communication: combine algorithmic transparency and decision rationale documentation with intuitive UI, helpful feedback, and cognitive-load-aware presentation to foster calibrated trust and adoption.
limitations: Small, single-organization sample; exploratory card-sorting captures perceptions rather than behavior; mixed-language sessions (interpreter) may affect nuance; limited generalizability to other industrial contexts.

title: Toward explainable AI (XAI) for mental health detection based on language behavior
domain: clinical NLP; mental health detection; explainable AI; digital phenotyping
task: build text-based classifiers for five mental health conditions and compare accuracy-interpretability trade-offs; identify informative feature groups and explanations
model: BiLSTM with human-interpretable feature sets; transformer baseline (domain-specific pretrained transformer, e.g., MentalRoBERTa) plus multi-task fusion (emotion/personality)
data_modality: social media text (Reddit) + engineered linguistic features + lexicon-based emotion/sentiment dictionaries
explanation_techniques: feature ablation with SP-LIME (global explanation) over interpretable feature groups; local LIME; AGRAD self-explaining attributions; word-importance mapped to LIWC categories; multi-task fusion learning for emotion/personality enrichment
evaluation_design: offline experiments; reports accuracy/precision/recall/F1; ablation analyses and explanation analyses across five binary tasks (ADHD, anxiety, bipolar, depression, stress)
metrics_performance: accuracy, precision, recall, F1 across tasks; compares interpretable BiLSTM vs transformer; multi-task fusion accuracy gains
main_findings: Interpretable BiLSTM models trained on handcrafted feature sets achieve moderate performance (e.g., BiLSTM F1 roughly 57–71 across tasks, with best for stress) but trail the black-box transformer by ~7.5 F1 on average. Feature ablation indicates different linguistic feature groups matter by condition (e.g., cohesion important for anxiety/depression; lexical features for bipolar). Multi-task fusion that injects emotion/personality information yields small average accuracy improvement (~+1.39%) over the transformer baseline, with larger gains for ADHD (emotion) and anxiety/bipolar (personality).
conclusions: Shows a practical path to increase explainability in mental health NLP: use interpretable feature-based models plus systematic ablation, and augment transformer models with auxiliary-domain information and attribution analyses to better understand decision factors; positions linguistic markers as digital biomarkers for early detection and monitoring.
limitations: Social-media-only data; labels differ across datasets (self-disclosure vs annotators) and may bias difficulty; primarily binary classification; limited generalizability to clinical settings; explanation methods have known limitations and require careful interpretation.

title: Explainable AI in Fintech Risk Management
domain: fintech; credit risk; P2P lending; explainable AI; regulatory compliance
user_group: financial risk managers/regulators (target audience); SME borrowers (data)
task: predict SME default risk for P2P lending and provide per-instance explanations
model: XGBoost (compared against logistic regression baseline) with SHAP/Shapley value explanations
data_modality: firm balance-sheet variables (SME financial data) + default label 1 year later
explanation_techniques: SHAP (Shapley additive explanations); per-instance Shapley decomposition; suggests clustering Shapley values for borrower segments
evaluation_design: train/test split (80/20); evaluate via ROC/AUC and classification metrics; compares XGBoost vs best logistic regression
participant_type: SMEs (Southern Europe; 2015 financial statements; default status in 2016)
metrics_performance: AUROC: logistic 0.81 vs XGBoost 0.93 (test set); default rate 10.9%
main_findings: XGBoost substantially improves AUROC (0.93 vs 0.81) while SHAP explanations reveal which balance-sheet variables drive each company’s risk score; explanations differ across companies, supporting individualized rationale rather than only global feature importance.
conclusions: Shows that post-hoc Shapley/SHAP explanations can make high-performing credit-risk models more suitable for regulated fintech by enabling stakeholder-understandable “main drivers” and auditability without sacrificing accuracy.
limitations: Limited to one dataset/time period and one credit-scoring context; explanations are post-hoc and do not guarantee causal “why”; dataset availability is on request; further work suggested on clustering and Shapley-based model selection/testing.

title: Explainable Model Fusion for Customer Journey Mapping
domain: marketing analytics; explainable alignment; process mining; human-AI decision support
user_group: marketing strategists / decision-makers (target audience; no user study)
task: automate customer journey mapping (CJM) and produce explainable representations for strategic decision-making
model: model-fusion pipeline combining LDA and VAE preprocessing with HMM + LFIT (GULA) rule extraction and LSTM sequential prediction
data_modality: OTT media viewing logs (touchpoints) and derived latent variables
explanation_techniques: post-hoc explainability via HMM state transitions; rule extraction using LFIT/GULA; interaction graph; latent attitude visualization via LDA+VAE; explainable alignment framework
interaction_design: n/a (no interactive user evaluation reported)
evaluation_design: offline experiment on broadcast/OTT viewing logs; evaluates prediction accuracy (Hamming distance), matched prediction under thresholds, and fidelity between LSTM predictions and HMM simulations
metrics_performance: Hamming distance accuracy; matched prediction rate vs thresholds; fidelity (LSTM vs HMM)
main_findings: Uses 1,058 viewers for training and 353 for testing; selects topic count 7; reports comparative accuracy/fidelity curves and interaction graphs/rules that relate explanatory variables to viewing dynamics. The fusion produces a CJM (HMM) plus symbolic rules intended to be readable by stakeholders, supporting consensus-building in strategic marketing decisions.
conclusions: Model-level fusion can bridge accuracy and understandability for strategic decision-making by producing CJM artifacts (state-transition diagrams) and symbolic rules aligned with stakeholder literacies; provides a concrete pipeline for “explainable alignment” in marketing contexts.
limitations: No human-subject evaluation of interpretability or decision impact; several hyperparameter/structure choices remain unresolved (e.g., LDA/VAE explainability); limited to one OTT/broadcast case study and specific data assumptions.

title: Yes we care!-Certification for machine learning methods through the care label framework
domain: trustworthy ML; certification/testing; safety-critical and resource-constrained ML deployment
user_group: ML deployers/customers without ML expertise; regulators; engineers
task: propose and evaluate a “care label” framework to certify ML method implementations against theoretical properties and bounds
model: framework for ML method certification; demonstrated on probabilistic graphical models (Markov random fields) with exact Junction Tree vs approximate Loopy Belief Propagation
data_modality: PGM inference outputs; system/compute resource measurements (runtime/memory/energy)
explanation_techniques: care labels for guarantees (reliability bounds, resource constraints); connects theory-to-implementation testing; demonstrated checks for probabilistic graphical model inference (accuracy via KL divergence thresholds; convergence checks); suggests extension to robustness and carbon/energy tracking
evaluation_design: framework + proof-of-concept verification suite + experimental evaluation comparing JT vs LBP inference and reporting label summaries (runtime/memory/energy/reliability grades)
metrics_trust: guaranteed properties as basis for trust without interpretability; certification for safety-critical/resource-constrained use
main_findings: Proposes care labels as a communication and certification mechanism for ML methods that enables non-experts to rely on tested guarantees, bridging ML theory and real implementations. Demonstrates feasibility on Markov random fields by testing inference implementations for compliance with theoretical properties and data fit, generating labels that expose trade-offs (exact JT vs approximate LBP) across reliability and resource consumption. Highlights scalability as an open challenge because expert knowledge databases and criteria checks are currently assembled manually; envisions long-term automation of tests from published proofs/experiments.
conclusions: Concludes care labels can inform diverse stakeholders about safety-critical and resource-constrained properties and can certify implementations via tests grounded in theory; demonstrates practical feasibility and motivates building and releasing verification suites and expert knowledge databases.
limitations: Scalability is a major limitation: expert knowledge databases and criteria checks require manual assembly/implementation; demonstrated primarily on PGMs (MRFs) and extending to other architectures (e.g., deep nets/robustness) adds complexity.

title: Explainability as the key ingredient for AI adoption in Industry 5.0 settings
domain: Industry 5.0; manufacturing XAI adoption; human-AI collaboration; platform engineering
user_group: manufacturing stakeholders; data scientists; domain experts; shop-floor operators (target users)
task: build and deploy the XMANAI platform to support explainable-by-design AI pipelines, collaboration, and lifecycle management; validate business value across industrial pilots
model: XMANAI platform (data/services/models layers; XAI pipeline execution + insight services; secure asset sharing; marketplace)
data_modality: manufacturing/operations data (pilot demonstrators); XAI pipelines; dashboards; evaluation questionnaires
explanation_techniques: explainable-by-design model families + XAI algorithms within a platform; evaluation combines extended 6P methodology (People pillar dimensions: Teaming, AI Integration, Explainability, AI Development) and business-value modeling via Fuzzy Cognitive Maps (FCMs)
interaction_design: n/a (platform provides collaboration via visualization engine/dashboards; supports data scientist–domain expert teaming)
evaluation_design: applies the approach to four industrial pilot demonstrators; evaluation via online AS-IS vs TO-BE questionnaire plus one-to-one meetings; radar charts; FCM simulations linking technical/XAI metrics to business KPIs
metrics_trust: trust/understandability captured as evaluation dimensions under People pillar (Explainability; Teaming)
metrics_performance: KPIs via 6P/FCM framework; technical metrics (e.g., MAPE) and their simulated effects on business KPIs
main_findings: XMANAI defines a reference architecture (cloud + on-premise) with multiple service bundles; provides pilot demonstrators (e.g., Ford, Whirlpool, CNH) and an evaluation framework. The extended 6P framework explicitly adds XAI-related human factors (Teaming/Explainability) and uses questionnaires to assess maturity shifts. FCM simulations illustrate how worse model error (MAPE) can reduce AI teaming via frustration/distrust and negatively affect KPIs.
conclusions: Proposes explainability as a key enabler for Industry 5.0 adoption by resolving the transparency paradox with a secure, collaborative platform plus an evaluation methodology that connects technical/XAI metrics to socio-business outcomes.
limitations: Proprietary pilot datasets limit reproducibility; several evaluation elements are illustrative (FCM simulation example not a real-world reflection); further operational validation beyond the four pilots and other contexts is needed.

title: Model-agnostic explainable artificial intelligence tools for severity prediction and symptom analysis on Indian COVID-19 data
domain: clinical decision support; COVID-19 severity prediction; model-agnostic XAI; trust in healthcare
user_group: healthcare stakeholders (target audience); patients in dataset (India)
task: predict COVID-19 severity from symptom/metadata features and interpret key drivers using XAI
model: Decision Tree, XGBoost, and Artificial Neural Network classifiers with SHAP and LIME explanations
data_modality: binary symptom/features derived from Indian patient records/datasets (symptoms + gender + contact); severity label (binary)
explanation_techniques: SHAP (global bar, local bar, beeswarm, waterfall, force plots); LIME (local bar, violin); feature-importance interpretation and per-patient local explanations
evaluation_design: offline experiments with resampling (ADASYN oversampling); reports accuracy/precision/recall/F1/AUC; compares 3 models and provides explanation case studies
participant_type: Indian COVID-19 cases aggregated from multiple sources (Jan 2020–Jan 2022; binary features)
metrics_performance: Best AUC among models is XGBoost (AUC 0.845; accuracy after resampling 86.895; F1 0.859); DT AUC 0.829; ANN AUC 0.805
main_findings: XGBoost outperforms DT/ANN on AUC and F1; SHAP/LIME explanations highlight influential symptoms/features for severity prediction (discussion notes Dry-Cough and Difficulty-in-Breathing as key across models). Paper emphasizes that higher AUC does not necessarily imply “most explainable” and compares global vs local XAI behavior.
conclusions: Illustrates how model-agnostic XAI can add human-interpretable evidence to healthcare severity models and support more trustworthy deployment, especially in under-studied contexts (Indian datasets).
limitations: Dataset construction merges multiple repositories and uses simplified binary features; class imbalance addressed via oversampling; limited clinical covariates; generalizability and clinical validity not fully established; explanations are post-hoc and sensitive (e.g., LIME perturbations).
